greek_head: Τίς ἦν ὦ Σώκρατες ᾧ χθὲς ἐν Λυκείῳ διελέγου ἦ πολὺς ὑμᾶς ὄχλος περιειστήκει ὥστ' ἔγωγε βουλόμενος
greek_tokens: tis eimi eimi swkraths os xqes en lukeion dialegw eimi polus su oxlos periisthmi wste egw boulomai akouw proserxomai oudeis oios su eimi akew safhs uperkuptw mentoi kateidon kai egw dokew eimi cenos tis os dialegw tis eimi poteros kai erwtaw eimi kritwn ou gar eis alla duh eimi os men egw legos ek decia tritos apo sew kaqhmai
english_head: Who was it, Socrates, that you were talking with yesterday at the Lyceum? Why, there was such a crowd standing about you that when I came up in the hope of listening I could hear nothing distinctly: still, by craning over I got a glimpse, and it appeared to me that it was a stranger with whom you were talking. Who was he? About which are you asking, Crito? There were two of them, not one. The man whom I mean was sitting next but one to you, on your right:

greek_head: ἐν μέσῳ δ' ὑμῶν τὸ Ἀξιόχου μειράκιον ἦν καὶ μάλα πολύ ὦ Σώκρατες ἐπιδεδωκέναι μοι ἔδοξεν καὶ τοῦ ἡμετέρου οὐ
greek_tokens: en mesos de su o acioxos meirakion eimi kai mala polus eimi swkraths epididwmi egw dokew kai o hmeteros ou polus tis o hlikia diaferw kritoboulos alla ekeinos men sklhfros outos de proferhs kai kalos kai agaqos o oyis euqudhmos outos eimi eimi kritwn os erwtaw o de para egw kaqhmai ek aristeros adelfos outos dionusodwros metexw de kai outos o logos oudeteros gignwskw eimi swkraths kainos tis au
english_head: between you was Axiochus’ boy; and he, Socrates, seemed to me to have grown a great deal, so as to look almost the same age as my Critobulus, who is rather puny whereas this boy has come on finely, and has a noble air about him. Euthydemus is the person to whom you refer, Crito, and the one sitting on my left was his brother, Dionysodorus. He too takes part in our discussions. Neither of them is known to me, Socrates. A pair of fresh additions,

greek_head: οὗτοι ὡς ἔοικε σοφισταί ποδαποί καὶ τίς ἡ σοφία Οὗτοι τὸ μὲν γένος ὡς ἐγᾦμαι ἐντεῦθέν ποθέν
greek_tokens: outos ws eoika sofisths podapos kai tis o sofia outos o men genos ws oimh enteuqen pinw eimi ek xion apoikew de eis qourios feugw de ekeiqen polus hdh eths peri ode o topos diatribw o de su erwtaw o sofia autos qaumasios eimi kritwn pansofos atexnos o ge oude oida pro o ostis eien e pagkratiasths outos gar eimi komidh pammaxos ou kata o akarnan gignomai o pagkratiasths adelfos ekeinos
english_head: I suppose, to our sophists. Where do they hail from, and what science do they profess? By birth I believe they belong to these parts, that is to say, Chios; they went out as colonists to Thurii, but have been exiled thence and have spent a good many years now in various parts of this country. As to what you ask of their profession, it is a wonderful one, Crito. These two men are absolutely omniscient: I never knew before what all-round sportsmen were. They are a pair of regular all-round fighters—not in the style of the famous all-round athletes, the two brothers of Acamania;

greek_head: μὲν γὰρ τῷ σώματι μόνον οἵω τε μάχεσθαι τούτω δὲ πρῶτον μὲν τῷ σώματι δεινοτάτω ἐστὸν καὶ μάχῃ ᾗ πάντων ἔστι
greek_tokens: men gar o swma monos oios te maxomai outos de proteros men o swma deinos eimi kai maxh h pas eimi kratew en oplon gar autos te sofos panu maxomai kai
english_head: they could fight with their bodies only. But these two, in the first place, are most formidable in body and in fight against all comers —for they are not only well skilled themselves in fighting under arms,

greek_head: ἄλλον ὃς ἂν διδῷ μισθόν οἵω τε ποιῆσαι ἔπειτα τὴν ἐν τοῖς δικαστηρίοις μάχην κρατίστω καὶ ἀγωνίσασθαι καὶ
greek_tokens: allos os an didwmi misqos oios te poiew epeita o en o dikasthrion maxh kratistos kai agwnizomai kai allos didaskw legw te kai suggrafw logos oios eis o dikasthrion pro o men oun outos deinos eimi monos nun de telos epitiqhmi pagkratiastikos texnh o gar eimi loipos autos maxh argos outos nun ecergazomai wste mhde an enos autos oios su eimi mhde antairw outws deinos gignomai en o logos maxomai te kai ecelegxw
english_head: but are able to impart that skill, for a fee, to another; and further, they are most competent also to fight the battle of the law-courts and teach others how to speak, or to have composed for them, such speeches as may win their suits. Formerly they had merely some ability for this; but now they have put the finishing touch to their skill as all-round sportsmen. The one feat of fighting yet unperformed by them they have now accomplished, so that nobody dares stand up to them for a moment; such a faculty they have acquired for wielding words as their weapons and confuting any argument as readily

greek_head: τὸ ἀεὶ λεγόμενον ὁμοίως ἐάντε ψεῦδος ἐάντε ἀληθὲς ᾖ ἐγὼ μὲν οὖν ὦ Κρίτων ἐν νῷ ἔχω τοῖν ἀνδροῖν παραδοῦναι
greek_tokens: o aei legw omoios ean yeudos ean alhqhs eimi egw men oun eimi kritwn en egw exw o anhr paradidwmi emautou kai gar fhmi en oligos xronos poiew an kai allos ostis o autos outos deinos tis de eimi swkraths ou fobew o hlikia mh hdh presbus eimi hkistos ge eimi kritwn ikanos tekmhrion exw kai paramuqion o mh fobew autos gar outos ws epos eipon gerwn eimi arxw outos o sofia eis egw epiqumew o eristikos perusi h properusi oudepw eimi
english_head: if it be true as if it be false. And so I, Crito, am minded to place myself in these two gentlemen’s hands; for they say it would take them but a little while to make anyone else clever in just the same way. What, Socrates! Are you not afraid, at your time of life, that you may be too old for that now? Not at all, Crito: I have enough proof and reassurance to the contrary. These same two persons were little less than old men at the time of their taking up this science, which I desire to have, of disputation.

greek_head: σοφώ ἀλλ' ἐγὼ ἓν μόνον φοβοῦμαι μὴ αὖ ὄνειδος τοῖν ξένοιν περιάψω ὥσπερ Κόννῳ τῷ Μητροβίου τῷ κιθαριστῇ
greek_tokens: sofos alla egw eis monos fobew mh au oneidos o cenos periaptw wsper konnos o mhtrobios o kiqarisths os egw didaskw eti kai nun kiqarizw oraw oun e pais e sumfoithths egw egw te katagelaw kai o konnos kalew gerontodidaskalos mh oun kai o cenos tis tautos outos oneidizw e de autos outos isos fobew taxa egw ou an eqelw prosdexomai egw de eimi kritwn ekeise men allos peiqw summaqhths egw foiths presbuths
english_head: Last year, or the year before, they were as yet without their science. The only thing I am afraid of is that I may bring the same disgrace upon our two visitors as upon Connus, son of Metrobius, the harper, who is still trying to teach me the harp; so that the boys who go to his lessons with me make fun of me and call Connus the gaffers’ master. This makes me fear that someone may make the same reproach to the two strangers; and, for aught I know, their dread of this very thing may make them unwilling to accept me. So, Crito, just as in the other case I have persuaded some elderly men to come and have lessons with me,

greek_head: ἐνταῦθα δέ γε ἑτέρους πειράσομαι πείθειν καὶ σὺ τί οὐ συμφοιτᾷς ὡς δὲ δέλεαρ αὐτοῖς ἄξομεν τοὺς σοὺς ὑεῖς
greek_tokens: entauqa de ge eteros peiraw peiqw kai su tis ou sumfoitaw ws de delear autos agnumi o sos uios efihmi gar ekeinos oida ostis kai egw paideuw alla oudeis kwluw eimi swkraths ean ge sos dokazw proteros de egw dihgeomai o sofia o anhr tis eimi ina oida ostis kai manqanw ou an fqanw akouw ws ou an exw ge eipon ostis ou prosexw o new autos alla panu kai prosexw kai mimnhskw kai su peiraw ek arxh apas dihgeomai
english_head: in this affair I am going to try and persuade another set. Now you, I am sure, will come with me to school; and we will take your sons as a bait to entice them, for I have no doubt that the attraction of these young fellows will make them include us also in the class. I have no objection, Socrates, if you think fit to do so. But first you must explain to me what is the science these men profess, that I may know what it is we are going to learn. You shall be told at once; for I cannot plead that I did not give them my attention, since I not only attended closely but remember and will try

greek_head: κατὰ θεὸν γάρ τινα ἔτυχον καθήμενος ἐνταῦθα οὗπερ σύ με εἶδες ἐν τῷ ἀποδυτηρίῳ μόνος καὶ ἤδη ἐν νῷ
greek_tokens: kata qeos gar tis tugxanw kaqhmai entauqa ouper su egw eidon en o apoduthrion monos kai hdh en egw exw anisthmi anisthmi de egw gignomai o eqw shmeion o daimonion palin oun katakaqezomai kai oligos
english_head: to expound the whole thing from the beginning. By some providence I chanced to be sitting in the place where you saw me, in the undressing-room, alone, and was just intending to get up and go; but the moment I did so, there came my wonted spiritual sign. So I sat down again,

greek_head: ὕστερον εἰσέρχεσθον τούτω ὅ τ' Εὐθύδημος καὶ ὁ Διονυσόδωρος καὶ ἄλλοι μαθηταὶ ἅμα αὖ πολλοὶ ἐμοὶ δοκεῖν
greek_tokens: usteron eiserxomai outos o su euqudhmos kai o dionusodwros kai allos maqhths ama au polus egw dokew eiserxomai de peripatew en o katastegos dromos kai oupw outos duh h trew dromos perierxomai eimi kai eiserxomai kleinihs os su fhmi polus epididwmi alhqhs legos opisqen de autos erasths panu polus te kai allos kai kthsippos neaniskos tis paianieus mala kalos te agaqos o fus osos mh ubrisths de dia o
english_head: and after a little while these two persons entered—Euthydemus and Dionysodorus—and accompanying them, quite a number, as it seemed to me, of their pupils: the two men came in and began walking round inside the cloister. Hardly had they taken two or three turns, when in stepped Cleinias, who you say has come on so much, and you are right: behind him was a whole troop of lovers, and among them Ctesippus, a young fellow from Paeania, of gentle birth and breeding, except for a certain insolence of youth.

greek_head: νέος εἶναι ἰδὼν οὖν με ὁ Κλεινίας ἀπὸ τῆς εἰσόδου μόνον καθήμενον ἄντικρυς ἰὼν παρεκαθέζετο ἐκ δεξιᾶς ὥσπερ καὶ
greek_tokens: neos eimi eidon oun egw o kleinihs apo o eisodos monos kaqhmai antikrus eimi parakaqezomai ek decia wsper kai su fhmi eidon de autos o te dionusodwros kai o euqudhmos proteros men efisthmi dialegw allhlwn allos kai allos apoblepw eis egw kai gar panu autos prosexw o new epeita eimi o men para o meirakion katakaqezomai o euqudhmos o de para autos egw ek aristeros e de allos ws ekastos tugxanw
english_head: So when Cleinias as he entered caught sight of me sitting there alone, he came straight across and sat beside me on my right, just as you say. Dionysodorus and Euthydemus, when they saw him, stood at first talking with each other, and casting an occasional glance at us—for my attention was fixed on them—but then one of them, Euthydemus, took a seat by the youth, and the other next to me on my left; the rest, where each happened to find one.

greek_head: Ἠσπαζόμην οὖν αὐτὼ ἅτε διὰ χρόνου ἑωρακώς μετὰ δὲ τοῦτο εἶπον πρὸς τὸν Κλεινίαν Ὦ Κλεινία τώδε μέντοι τὼ
greek_tokens: aspazomai oun autos ate dia xronos oraw meta de outos eipon pros o kleinihs eimi kleinia ode mentoi o anhr sofos euqudhmos te kai dionusodwros ou o mikros alla o megas o gar peri o polemos pas efisthmi osos deomai o mellw agaqos strathgos eimi o te tacis kai o hgemonia o stratopedon kai osos en oplon maxomai didakteon oios te de kai poiew dunatos eimi autos eautou bohqew en o dikasthrion an tis autos adikew
english_head: So I greeted the two brothers, as not having seen them for some time; after that I said to Cleinias: My dear Cleinias, these two men, you know, are skilled not in little things, but in great. For they understand all about war, that is, as much as is needful for him who is to be a good general; both the tactics and the strategy of armies, and all the teaching of troops under arms; and they can also enable one to get redress in the law courts for a wrong that one may have suffered. When I had said this,

greek_head: Εἰπὼν οὖν ταῦτα κατεφρονήθην ὑπ' αὐτοῖν ἐγελασάτην οὖν ἄμφω βλέψαντε εἰς ἀλλήλω καὶ ὁ Εὐθύδημος εἶπεν
greek_tokens: eipon oun outos katafronew upo autos gelaw oun amfw blepw eis allhlwn kai o euqudhmos eipon outoi eti outos eimi swkraths spoudazw alla parergon autos xraomai agos qaumazw eipon kalos an pou o ergnumi su eaw ei thlikoutos pragma parergon su tugxanw eimi kai pros qea eipon egw tis eimi outos o kalos areth fhmi eimi swkraths oiomai oios su eimi paradidwmi kallista anqrwpos kai taxistos
english_head: I saw they despised me for it, and they both laughed, looking at each other; then Euthydemus said: No, no, Socrates, we do not make those matters our business now; we deal with them as diversions. At this I wondered and said: Your business must be a fine one, if such great matters are indeed diversions to you; so I beseech you, tell me what this fine business is. Virtue, Socrates, he replied, is what we deem ourselves able to purvey in a pre-eminently excellent and speedy manner.

greek_head: Ὦ Ζεῦ οἷον ἦν δ' ἐγώ λέγετον πρᾶγμα πόθεν τοῦτο τὸ ἕρμαιον ηὑρέτην ἐγὼ δὲ περὶ ὑμῶν διενοούμην ἔτι ὥσπερ
greek_tokens: eimi zew oios eimi de egw legw pragma pinw outos o ermaion euriskw egw de peri su dianoeomai eti wsper nundi elegos ws o polus outos deinos eimi en oplon maxomai kai outos elegos peri sfeis oste gar o proteros epidhmew outos mimnhskw sfeis epaggellw ei de nun alhqhs outos o episthmh exw ilaos eimi atexnos gar egw sfeis wsper qeos prosagoreuw suggnwmh
english_head: Good heavens, I exclaimed, a mighty affair indeed! Where did you have the luck to pick it up? I was still considering you, as I remarked just now, to be chiefly skilled in fighting under arms, and so spoke of you in those terms: for when you visited, our city before, this, I recollect, was the profession you made. But if you now in truth possess this other knowledge, have mercy—you see I address you just as though you were a couple of gods, beseeching you to forgive my former remarks.

greek_head: δεόμενος ἔχειν μοι τῶν ἔμπροσθεν εἰρημένων ἀλλ' ὁρᾶτον ὦ Εὐθύδημέ τε καὶ Διονυσόδωρε εἰ ἀληθῆ λέγετον
greek_tokens: deomai exw egw o emprosqen erw alla oraw eimi euqudhmos te kai dionusodwros ei alhqhs legw upo gar o megeqos o epaggelma oudeis qaumastos apistew alla eu eimi eimi swkraths fhmi outos outws exw makarizw ara su egw o kthma polus mallon h megas basileus o arxh tososde de egw eipon ei en egw exw epideiknumi outos o sofia h pws sfeis bouleuw epi autos ge outos pareimi eimi swkraths ws epideiknumi
english_head: But make sure, Euthydemus and Dionysodorus, that you spoke the truth: for the vastness of your promise gives me some excuse for disbelieving. You may be sure, Socrates, they replied, it is as we say. Then I congratulate you on your acquisition far more than I do the great king on his empire: only tell me whether you intend to exhibit this science of yours, or what you have determined to do. We are here for the very purpose, Socrates, of exhibiting

greek_head: καὶ διδάξοντε ἐάν τις ἐθέλῃ μανθάνειν Ἀλλ' ὅτι μὲν ἐθελήσουσιν ἅπαντες οἱ μὴ ἔχοντες ἐγὼ
greek_tokens: kai didaskw ean tis eqelw manqanw alla ostis men eqelw apas e mh exw egw su egguaw proteros men egw epeita de kleinihs outos pros de egw kthsippos te ode kai e allos outos eimi de egw deiknumi autos o erasths o kleinihs e de tugxanw egw hdh periisthmi o gar kthsippos tugxanw prosw kataezomai o kleinihs amos dokew ws tugxanw o euqudhmos egw dialegw proneuw eis o prosqen
english_head: and expounding it to anyone who wishes to learn. Well, I guarantee that all who do not possess it will wish to—myself to begin with, then Cleinias here and, besides us, Ctesippus and all these others, I said, showing him the lovers of Cleinias, who were by this time standing about us. For Ctesippus, as it happened, was sitting some way from Cleinias, I noticed; and by chance, as Euthydemus leant forward

greek_head: ἐν μέσῳ ὄντος ἡμῶν τοῦ Κλεινίου ἐπεσκότει τῷ Κτησίππῳ τῆς θέας βουλόμενός τε οὖν θεάσασθαι ὁ Κτήσιππος τὰ
greek_tokens: en mesos eimi egw o kleinihs episkotew o kthsippos o qea boulomai te oun qeaomai o kthsippos o paidikon kai ama filhkoos eimi anaphdaw proteros prosisthmi egw en o katantikru outws oun kai e allos ekeinos eidon periisthmi egw e te o kleinihs erasths kai e o euqudhmos te kai dionusodwros etairos outos dh egw deiknumi elegos o euqudhmos ostis pas etoimos eien
english_head: in talking to me he obscured Ctesipus’ view of Cleinias, who was between us. Then Ctesippus, desiring to gaze on his favorite and being also an eager listener, led the way by jumping up and placing himself opposite us; and this made the others, on seeing what he did, stand around us, both Cleinias’ lovers and the followers of Euthydemus and Dionysodorus. Pointing to these, I told Euthydemus that they were all ready to learn; to which Ctesippus assented with great eagerness,

greek_head: μανθάνειν ὅ τε οὖν Κτήσιππος συνέφη μάλα προθύμως καὶ οἱ ἄλλοι καὶ ἐκέλευον αὐτὼ κοινῇ πάντες ἐπιδείξασθαι
greek_tokens: manqanw o te oun kthsippos sumfhmi mala proqumos kai e allos kai keleuw autos koinh pas epideiknumi o dunamis o sofia eipon oun egw eimi euqudhmos kai dionusodwros panu men oun pas tropos kai outos xarizomai kai egw eneka epideiknumi o men oun pleistos dhlos ostis ou oligos ergnumi epideiknumi ode de egw eipon poteros peiqw hdh ws xrh para su manqanw dunamai an agaqos poiew
english_head: and so did the rest; and they all joined in urging the two men to exhibit the power of their wisdom. On this I remarked: My good Euthydemus and Dionysodorus, you must do your very best to gratify my friends and, for my sake also, to give us an exhibition. To do it in full, of course, would obviously be a lengthy performance: but tell me one thing—will you be able to make a good man of him only who is already convinced that he should learn of you,

greek_head: ἄνδρα μόνον ἢ καὶ ἐκεῖνον τὸν μήπω πεπεισμένον διὰ τὸ μὴ οἴεσθαι ὅλως τὸ πρᾶγμα τὴν ἀρετὴν μαθητὸν εἶναι ἢ μὴ σφὼ
greek_tokens: anhr monos h kai ekeinos o mhpw peiqw dia o mh oiomai oloc o pragma o areth maqhtos eimi h mh sfeis eimi autos didaskalos ferw kai o outws exw o autos texnh ergnumi peiqw ws kai didaktos o areth kai outos su eimi para os an kallista tis autos manqanw h allos outos men oun fhmi o autos eimi swkraths o dionusodwros su ara eimi de egw eimi dionusodwros o nun anqrwpos
english_head: or of him also who is not yet so convinced, owing to an absolute disbelief that virtue is a thing that can be learnt or that you are teachers of it? Come now, is it the business of this same art to persuade such a man that virtue is teachable and that you are the men of whom one may best learn it, or does this need some other art? No, this same one can do it, Socrates, said Dionysodorus. Then you two, Dionysodorus, I said, would be the best persons now on earth to incite one to the pursuit of knowledge

greek_head: κάλλιστ' ἂν προτρέψαιτε εἰς φιλοσοφίαν καὶ ἀρετῆς ἐπιμέλειαν
greek_tokens: kallista an protrepw eis filosofia kai aretaw epimeleia oiomai ge dh eimi swkraths o men toinun allos o epideicis egw fhmi eis auqis apotiqhmi outos de autos epideiknumi outos o neaniskos peiqw ws xrh filosofew kai aretaw epimeleomai kai xarizomai egw te kai outos pas sumbainw gar tis toioutos o meirakion outos egw te kai ode pas tugxanw epiqumew ws beltistos autos gignomai eimi de outos acioxos men us o alkibiadhs o palaios autaneyios
english_head: and the practice of virtue? We think so, at least, Socrates. Well then, please defer the display of all the rest to some other occasion, I said, and exhibit this one thing. You are to persuade this young fellow here that he ought to ensue wisdom and practise virtue, and so you will oblige both me and all these present. This youth happens to be in just the sort of condition I speak of; and I and all of us here are at this moment anxious for him to become as good as possible. He is the son of Axiochus, son of the former Alcibiades,

greek_head: δὲ τοῦ νῦν ὄντος Ἀλκιβιάδου ὄνομα δ' αὐτῷ Κλεινίας ἔστι δὲ νέος φοβούμεθα δὴ περὶ αὐτῷ οἷον εἰκὸς περὶ νέῳ
greek_tokens: de o nun eimi alkibiadhs onoma de autos kleinihs eimi de neos fobew dh peri autos oios eoika peri neos mh tis fqanw egw epi allos tis epithdeuma trepw autos o dianoia kai diafqeirw sfeis oun hkw eis kalos alla ei mh tis diaferw su lambanw peirw o meirakion kai dialegw enantion egw eipon oun egw sxedon tis autos outos o euqudhmos ama andreios te kai qarsaleos alla oudeis diaferw eimi
english_head: and is own cousin to the Alcibiades that now is: his name is Cleinias. He is young; and so we have fears for him, as well one may for a young man, lest someone forestall us and turn his inclination to some other course of life, and so corrupt him. Hence your arrival now is most happy. Come now, if it is all the same to you, make trial of the lad and talk with him in our presence. When I had thus spoken, in almost these very words, Euthydemus answered in a tone both manly and dashing: Oh, it is all the same to us,

greek_head: Σώκρατες ἔφη ἐὰν μόνον ἐθέλῃ ἀποκρίνεσθαι ὁ νεανίσκος Ἀλλὰ μὲν δή ἔφην ἐγώ τοῦτό γε καὶ εἴθισται θαμὰ γὰρ
greek_tokens: swkraths fhmi ean monos eqelw apokrinw o neaniskos alla men dh fhmi egw outos ge kai eqizw qama gar autos ode proseimi polus erwtaw te kai dialegw wste epieikhs qarsew o apokrinw o dh meta outos eimi kritwn pws an kalos su dihgeomai ou gar mikros o ergnumi dunamai analambanw dieceimi sofia amhxanos osos wste egw kaqa e
english_head: Socrates, provided the youth is willing to answer us. Why, in fact, I said, that is just what he is used to: these people here are constantly coming to him and asking him a number of questions and debating with him, so he is a fairly fearless answerer. What ensued, Crito, how am I to relate in proper style? For no slight matter it is to be able to recall in description such enormous knowledge as theirs.

greek_head: ποιηταί δέομαι ἀρχόμενος τῆς διηγήσεως Μούσας τε καὶ Μνημοσύνην ἐπικαλεῖσθαι ἤρξατο δ' οὖν ἐνθένδε ποθὲν
greek_tokens: poihths deomai arxw o dihghsis mousa te kai mnhmosunh epikalew arxw de oun enqende pinw o euqudhmos ws oimh eimi kleinia poteros eimi o anqrwpos e manqanw e sofos h e amaqhs kai o meirakion ate megas eimi o erwthma eruqriaw te kai aporew emblepw eis egw kai egw gignwskw autos qorubew qarsos eimi de egw eimi kleinia
english_head: Consequently, like the poets, I must needs begin my narrative with an invocation of the Muses and Memory. Well, Euthydemus set to work, so far as I remember, in terms very much the same as these: Cleinias, which sort of men are the learners, the wise or the foolish? At this the young man, feeling the embarrassment of the question, blushed and glanced at me in his helplessness. So I, perceiving his confusion, said: Have no fear, Cleinias; answer bravely,

greek_head: καὶ ἀπόκριναι ἀνδρείως ὁπότερά σοι φαίνεται ἴσως γάρ τοι ὠφελεῖ τὴν μεγίστην ὠφελίαν
greek_tokens: kai apokrinw andreios opoteros su fainw isos gar su wfelew o megas wfeleia kai en outos o dionusodwros proskuptw egw mikros pros o ous panu meidiaw o proswpon kai meis fhmi sos eimi swkraths prolegw ostis opoteros an apokrinw o meirakion ecelegxw kai autos metacu outos legw o kleinihs tugxanw apokrinw wste oude parakeleuomai egw ekgignomai
english_head: whichever you think it is: for perchance he is doing you the greatest service in the world. Meanwhile Dionysodorus leant over a little to me, with a broad smile on his face, and whispered in my ear: Let me tell you, Socrates, beforehand that, whichever way the lad answers, he will be confuted. While he was saying this, Cleinias made his reply, so that I was unable even to advise

greek_head: εὐλαβηθῆναι τῷ μειρακίῳ ἀλλ' ἀπεκρίνατο ὅτι οἱ σοφοὶ εἶεν οἱ μανθάνοντες
greek_tokens: eulabeomai o meirakion alla apokrinw ostis e sofos eien e manqanw kai o euqudhmos kalew de tis fhmi didaskalos h ou omologew oukoun o manqanw e didaskalos didaskalos eimi wsper o kiqarisths kai o grammatisths didaskalos dhpou eimi sew kai o allos pais su de maqhths sumfhmi allos tis oun hnika manqanw oupw epistamai outos o manqanw ou fhmi ara oun
english_head: the boy to be wary: he replied that it was the wise who were the learners. Then Euthydemus asked: And are there persons whom you call teachers, or not? He agreed that there were. And the teachers of the learners are teachers in the same way as your lute-master and your writing-master, I suppose, were teachers of you and the other boys, while you were pupils? He assented. Now, of course, when you were learning, you did not yet know the things you were learning?

greek_head: σοφοὶ ἦτε ὅτε ταῦτα οὐκ ἠπίστασθε Οὐ δῆτα ἦ δ' ὅς Οὐκοῦν εἰ μὴ σοφοί ἀμαθεῖς Πάνυ γε Ὑμεῖς ἄρα
greek_tokens: sofos eimi oste outos ou epistamai ou dhta eimi de os oukoun ei mh sofos amaqhs panu ge su ara manqanw o ou epistamai amaqhs eimi manqanw epinew o meirakion e amaqhs ara manqanw eimi kleinia alla ou e sofos ws su oiomai outos oun eipon autos wsper upo didaskalos xoros aposhmainw ama anaqorubew te kai gelaw e epomai
english_head: No, he said. So were you wise, when you did not know those things? No, to be sure, he said. Then if not wise, foolish? Certainly. So when you learnt what you did not know, you learnt while being foolish. To this the lad nodded assent. Hence it is the foolish who learn, Cleinias, and not the wise, as you suppose. When he had thus spoken, all those followers of Dionysodorus

greek_head: ἐκεῖνοι μετὰ τοῦ Διονυσοδώρου τε καὶ Εὐθυδήμου καὶ πρὶν ἀναπνεῦσαι καλῶς τε καὶ εὖ τὸ μειράκιον ἐκδεξάμενος
greek_tokens: ekeinos meta o dionusodwros te kai euqudhmos kai prin anapnew kalos te kai eu o meirakion ekdexomai o dionusodwros tis de eimi kleinia fhmi opote apostomatizw su o grammatisths poteros manqanw o pais o apostomatizw e sofos h e amaqhs e sofos fhmi o kleinihs e sofos ara manqanw alla ou e amaqhs kai ou eu su arti euqudhmos apokrinw
english_head: and Euthydemus raised a cheer and a laugh, like a chorus at the signal of their director; and before the boy could fairly and fully recover his breath Dionysodorus took up the cudgels and said: Well now, Cleinias, whenever your writing-master dictated from memory, which of the boys learnt the piece recited, the wise or the foolish? The wise, said Cleinias. So it is the wise who learn, and not the foolish: hence the answer you gave just now to Euthydemus was a bad one.

greek_head: Ἐνταῦθα δὴ καὶ πάνυ μέγα ἐγέλασάν τε καὶ ἐθορύβησαν οἱ ἐρασταὶ τοῖν ἀνδροῖν ἀγασθέντες τῆς σοφίας αὐτοῖν οἱ
greek_tokens: entauqa dh kai panu megas gelaw te kai qorubew e erasths o anhr agamai o sofia autos e de allos egw ekplhssw siwpaw gignwskw de egw o euqudhmos ekplhssw ina eti mallon qaumazw autos ou anihmi o meirakion alla erwtaw kai wsper e agaqos orxhsths diplazw strefw o erwthma peri o autos kai fhmi poteros gar e manqanw manqanw o efisthmi h o mh efisthmi kai o dionusodwros palin mikros pros egw yiqurizw
english_head: Thereupon arose a great deal of laughter and loud applause from the pair’s adorers, in admiration of their cleverness; while we on our side were dismayed and held our peace. Then Euthydemus, observing our dismay, and seeking to astonish us still further, would not let the boy go, but went on questioning him and, like a skilful dancer, gave a twofold twist to his questions on the same point: Now, do the learners learn what they know, he asked, or what they do not? Then Dionysodorus whispered to me again softly:

greek_head: Καὶ τοῦτ' ἔφη ὦ Σώκρατες ἕτερον τοιοῦτον οἷον τὸ πρότερον
greek_tokens: kai outos fhmi eimi swkraths eteros toioutos oios o proteros eimi zew fhmi egw eimi meis kai o proteros ge kalos egw fainw o erwthma panth fhmi eimi swkraths toioutos egw erwtaw afuktos toigar eimi de egw dokew egw eudokimew para o maqhths en de outos o men kleinihs o euqudhmos apokrinw ostis manqanw e manqanw o ou efisthmi o de airw
english_head: Here comes a second one, Socrates, just like the first. Heavens! I replied: surely the first question served you well enough. All our questions, Socrates, he said, are like that; they leave no escape. And consequently, as it seems to me, I remarked, you have this high repute among your disciples. Meanwhile Cleinias answered Euthydemus, that learners learnt what they did not know; so he had to meet the same course of questions as before:

greek_head: αὐτὸν διὰ τῶν αὐτῶν ὧνπερ τὸ πρότερον Τί δέ ἦ δ' ὅς οὐκ ἐπίστασαι σὺ γράμματα Ναί ἔφη Οὐκοῦν ἅπαντα
greek_tokens: autos dia o autos os o proteros tis de eimi de os ou efisthmi su gramma nai fhmi oukoun apas omologew otan oun tis apostomatizw otioun ou gramma apostomatizw omologew oukoun os tis su efisthmi fhmi apostomatizw eiper pas efisthmi kai outos omologew tis oun eimi de os ara su ou manqanw ostis an apostomatizw tis o de mh efisthmi gramma manqanw ou alla eimi de os manqanw oukoun o efisthmi eimi de
english_head: Well then, asked the other, do you not know your letters? Yes, he said. All of them? He admitted it. Now when anyone dictates some piece or other, does he not dictate letters? He admitted it. And he dictates things of which you know something, since you know all of them? He admitted this too. Well now, said the other, surely you do not learn whatever such a person dictates; it is rather he who does not know his letters that learns? No, he replied; I learn. Then you learn what you know, since you know all your letters.

greek_head: ὅς μανθάνεις εἴπερ γε ἅπαντα τὰ γράμματα ἐπίστασαι Ὡμολόγησεν Οὐκ ἄρα ὀρθῶς ἀπεκρίνω ἔφη
greek_tokens: os manqanw eiper ge apas o gramma efisthmi omologew ou ara orqos apokrinw fhmi kai oupw sfodra tis outos erw o euqudhmos kai o dionusodwros wsper sfaira ekdexomai o logos palin stoxazomai o meirakion kai eipon ecapataw su euqudhmos eimi kleinia eipon gar egw o manqanw ou episthmh eimi lambanw outos e an tis manqanw omologew o kleinihs o de efisthmi eimi de os allos tis h exw episthmh hdh eimi sumfhmi o ara mh efisthmi
english_head: He agreed. So your answer was not correct, he said. The last word was hardly out of Euthydemus’ mouth when Dionysodorus caught, as it were, the ball of the argument and, aiming at the boy again, said: Euthydemus is deceiving you, Cleinias. Tell me, is not learning the reception of knowledge of that which one learns? Cleinias agreed. And is not knowing, he went on, just having knowledge at the time? He assented. So that not knowing

greek_head: μήπω ἔχειν ἐπιστήμην ἐστίν Ὡμολόγει αὐτῷ Πότερον οὖν εἰσιν οἱ λαμβάνοντες ὁτιοῦν οἱ ἔχοντες ἤδη ἢ οἳ ἂν μὴ
greek_tokens: mhpw exw episthmh eimi omologew autos poteros oun eimi e lambanw otioun e exw hdh h e an mh exw e an mh oukoun omologew eimi outos kai o mh efisthmi o mh exw kataneuw o lambanw ara eimi e manqanw alla ou o exw sumfhmi e mh efisthmi ara fhmi manqanw eimi kleinia alla ou e efisthmi
english_head: is not yet having knowledge? He agreed with him. Then are those who receive anything those who have it already, or those who have it not? Those who have it not. And you have admitted that those who do not know belong also to this class of those who have it not? He nodded assent. And the learners belong to the class of the receiving and not to that of the having? He agreed. Hence it is those who do not know that learn, Cleinias, and not those who know. Euthydemus was proceeding to press the youth for the third fall, when I,

greek_head: Ἔτι δὴ ἐπὶ τὸ τρίτον καταβαλῶν ὥσπερ πάλαισμα ὥρμα ὁ Εὐθύδημος τὸν νεανίσκον καὶ ἐγὼ γνοὺς βαπτιζόμενον τὸ
greek_tokens: eti dh epi o tritos kataballw wsper palaisma ormaw o euqudhmos o neaniskos kai egw gignwskw baptizw o meirakion boulomai anapauw autos mh egw apodeiliaw paramuqeomai eipon eimi kleinia mh qaumazw ei su fainw ahqhs e logos isos gar ou aisqanomai oios poiew o cenos peri sos poiew de tautos osper e en o teleth o korubas otan o qronwsis poiew peri outos os an mellw tellw kai gar ekei xoreia tis eimi kai paidih ei ara kai telew kai nun
english_head: perceiving the lad was going under, and wishing to give him some breathing-space lest he should shame us by losing heart, encouraged him with these words: Cleinias, do not be surprised that these arguments seem strange to you; for perhaps you do not discern what our two visitors are doing to you. They are acting just like the celebrants of the Corybantic rites, when they perform the enthronement of the person whom they are about to initiate. There, as you know, if you have been through it, they have dancing and merrymaking: so here these two

greek_head: τούτω οὐδὲν ἄλλο ἢ χορεύετον περὶ σὲ καὶ οἷον ὀρχεῖσθον παίζοντε ὡς μετὰ τοῦτο τελοῦντε νῦν οὖν νόμισον τὰ
greek_tokens: outos oudeis allos h xoreuw peri sos kai oios orxeomai paizw ws meta outos tellw nun oun nomizw o proteros o iera akouw o sofistikos proteros gar os fhmi prodikos peri onoma orqoths manqanw deomai o dh kai endeiknumi su o cenos ostis ou oida o manqanw ostis e anqrwpos kalew men epi o toiosde otan tis ek arxh mhdeis exw episthmh peri pragma
english_head: are merely dancing about you and performing their sportive gambols with a view to your subsequent initiation. You must now, accordingly, suppose you are listening to the first part of the professorial mysteries. First of all, as Prodicus says, you have to learn about the correct use of words—the very point that our two visitors are making plain to you, namely, that you were unaware that learning is the name which people apply on the one hand to the case of a man who, having originally no knowledge about some matter, in course of time receives such knowledge;

greek_head: τινος ἔπειτα ὕστερον αὐτοῦ λαμβάνῃ τὴν ἐπιστήμην καλοῦσι δὲ ταὐτὸν τοῦτο καὶ ἐπειδὰν ἔχων ἤδη τὴν ἐπιστήμην ταύτῃ
greek_tokens: tis epeita usteron autos lambanw o episthmh kalew de tautos outos kai epeidan exw hdh o episthmh outos o episthmh tautos outos pragma episkopew h prassw h legw mallon men autos suneimi kalew h manqanw eimi de oste kai manqanw sos de outos ws outos endeiknumi dialanqanw tautos onoma epi anqrwpos enantios exw keimai o te oida kai epi o mh paraplhsios de outos kai o en o deuteros erwthma en os
english_head: and on the other hand the same word is applied when, having the knowledge already, he uses that knowledge for the investigation of the same matter whether occurring in action or in speech. It is true that they tend rather to call it understanding than learning, but occasionally they call it learning too; and this point, as our friends are demonstrating, has escaped your notice—how the same word is used for people who are in the opposite conditions of knowing and not knowing. A similar point underlay the second question, where they asked you

greek_head: ἠρώτων σε πότερα μανθάνουσιν οἱ ἄνθρωποι ἃ ἐπίστανται ἢ ἃ μή ταῦτα δὴ τῶν μαθημάτων παιδιά ἐστιν διὸ καί
greek_tokens: erwtaw su poteros manqanw e anqrwpos o efisthmi h o mh outos dh o maqhma paidih eimi dio kai fhmi egw su outos prospaizw paidih de legos dia outos ostis ei kai polus tis h kai pas o toioutos manqanw o men pragma oudeis an mallon oida ph exis prospaizw de oios su an eaw o anqrwpos dia o o onoma diafora uposkelizw kai anatrepw wsper e o skoluqrion o mellw kaqizanw upospaw xairw
english_head: whether people learn what they know, or what they do not. Such things are the sport of the sciences—and that is why I tell you these men are making game of you; I call it sport because, although one were to learn many or even all of such tricks, one would be not a whit the wiser as to the true state of the matters in hand, but only able to make game of people, thanks to the difference in the sense of the words, by tripping them up and overturning them; just as those who slyly pull stools away from persons who are about to sit down

greek_head: καὶ γελῶσιν ἐπειδὰν ἴδωσιν ὕπτιον ἀνατετραμμένον ταῦτα μὲν οὖν σοι παρὰ τούτων νόμιζε παιδιὰν γεγονέναι τὸ δὲ
greek_tokens: kai gelaw epeidan eidon uptios anatrepw outos men oun su para outos nomizw paidih gignomai o de meta outos dhlos ostis outos ge su autos o spoudaios endeiknumi kai egw ufhgeomai autos ina egw o upexw apodew fhmi gar epideiknumi o protreptikos sofia nun de egw dokew dew oiomai proteros pas pros sos outos men oun eimi euqudhmos te kai dionusodwros
english_head: make merry and laugh when they see one sprawling on one’s back. So far, then, you are to regard these gentlemen’s treatment of you as mere play: but after this they will doubtless display to you their own serious object, while I shall keep them on the track and see that they fulfil the promise they gave me. They said they would exhibit their skill in exhortation; but instead, I conceive, they thought fit to make sport with you first. So now, Euthydemus and Dionysodorus,

greek_head: πεπαίσθω τε ὑμῖν καὶ ἴσως ἱκανῶς ἔχει τὸ δὲ δὴ μετὰ ταῦτα ἐπιδείξατον προτρέποντε τὸ μειράκιον ὅπως χρὴ
greek_tokens: paiw te su kai isos ikanos exis o de dh meta outos epideiknumi protrepw o meirakion opws xrh sofia te kai aretaw epimeleomai proteros de egw sfeis endeiknumi oios autos upolambanw kai oios autos epiqumew akew ean oun docow su idiwtikos te kai geloios autos poiew mh egw katagelaw upo proqumia gar o akew o umeteros sofia tolmaw apoautosxediazw enantion
english_head: let us have done with your sport: I daresay you have had as much as you want. What you have next to do is to give us a display of exhorting this youth as to how he should devote himself to wisdom and virtue. But first I shall explain to you how I regard this matter and how I desire to hear it dealt with. If I strike you as treating it in a crude and ridiculous manner, do not laugh me to scorn; for in my eagerness

greek_head: ὑμῶν ἀνάσχεσθον οὖν ἀγελαστὶ ἀκούοντες αὐτοί τε καὶ οἱ μαθηταὶ ὑμῶν σὺ δέ μοι ὦ παῖ Ἀξιόχου ἀπόκριναι
greek_tokens: su anexw oun agelasti akouw autos te kai e maqhths su su de egw eimi pais acioxos apokrinw ara ge pas anqrwpos boulomai eu prassw h outos men erwthma os nundi fobew eis o katagelastos anohtos gar dhpou kai o erwtaw o toioutos tis gar ou boulomai anqrwpos eu prassw oudeis ostis ou fhmi
english_head: to listen to your wisdom I shall venture to improvise in your presence. So both you and your disciples must restrain yourselves and listen without laughing; and you, son of Axiochus, answer me this: Do all we human beings wish to prosper? Or is this question one of the absurdities I was afraid of just now? For I suppose it is stupid merely to ask such things, since every man must wish to prosper.

greek_head: ὁ Κλεινίας Εἶεν ἦν δ' ἐγώ τὸ δὴ μετὰ τοῦτο ἐπειδὴ βουλόμεθα εὖ πράττειν πῶς ἂν εὖ πράττοιμεν ἆρ' ἂν εἰ ἡμῖν
greek_tokens: o kleinihs eien eimi de egw o dh meta outos epei boulomai eu prassw pws an eu prassw ara an ei egw polus agaqos eaw h outos ekeinos eti euhqhs dhlos gar pou kai outos ostis outws exis sumfhmi ferw dh agaqos de poios ara o eimi tugxanw egw eimi h ou xalepos oude semnon anhr panu tis oude outos eoika eimi euporew pas gar an egw eipon ostis o ploutew agaqos eimi gar panu ge fhmi oukoun kai o ugiainw kai o
english_head: Everyone in the world, said Cleinias. Well then, I asked, as to the next step, since we wish to prosper, how can we prosper? Will it be if we have many good things? Or is this an even sillier question than the other? For surely this too must obviously be so. He agreed. Come now, of things that are, what sort do we hold to be really good? Or does it appear to be no difficult matter, and no problem for an important person, to find here too a ready answer? Anyone will tell us that to be rich is good, surely? Quite true, he said. Then it is the same with being healthy and handsome, and having the other bodily endowments

greek_head: καλὸν εἶναι καὶ τἆλλα κατὰ τὸ σῶμα ἱκανῶς παρεσκευάσθαι Συνεδόκει Ἀλλὰ μὴν εὐγένειαί γε καὶ δυνάμεις καὶ
greek_tokens: kalos eimi kai allos kata o swma ikanos paraskeuazw sundokew alla meis eugeneia ge kai dunamis kai timh en o eautou dhlos eimi agaqos eimi omologew tis oun fhmi eti egw leipw o agaqos tis ara eimi o swfrwn te eimi kai dikaios kai andreios poteros pros zeus eimi kleinia hgeomai su ean outos tiqhmi ws agaqos orqos egw tiqhmi h ean mh isos gar an tis egw amfisbhtew sos de pws dokew agaqos fhmi o kleinihs
english_head: in plenty? He agreed. Again, it is surely clear that good birth and talents and distinctions in one’s own country are good things. He admitted it. Then what have we still remaining, I asked, in the class of goods? What of being temperate, and just, and brave? I bay you tell me, Cleinias, do you think we shall be right in ranking these as goods, or in rejecting them? For it may be that someone will dispute it. How does it strike you? They are goods, said Cleinias. Very well, I went on,

greek_head: Εἶεν ἦν δ' ἐγώ τὴν δὲ σοφίαν ποῦ χοροῦ τάξομεν ἐν τοῖς ἀγαθοῖς ἢ πῶς λέγεις Ἐν τοῖς ἀγαθοῖς Ἐνθυμοῦ
greek_tokens: eien eimi de egw o de sofia pou xoros tassw en o agaqos h pws legw en o agaqos enqumeomai dh mh tis paraleipw o agaqos ostis kai acios logos alla egw dokew fhmi oudeis o kleinihs kai egw anamimnhskw eipon ostis nai ma dios kinduneuw ge o megas o agaqos paraleipw tis outos eimi de os o eutuxia eimi kleinia o pas fhmi kai e panu faulos megas o agaqos eimi alhqhs legw fhmi kai egw au palin metanoew eipon ostis oligos katagelastos
english_head: and where in the troupe shall we station wisdom? Among the goods, or how? Among the goods. Then take heed that we do not pass over any of the goods that may deserve mention. I do not think we are leaving any out, said Cleinias. Hereupon I recollected one and said: Yes, by Heaven, we are on the verge of omitting the greatest of the goods. What is that? he asked. Good fortune, Cleinias: a thing which all men, even the worst fools, refer to as the greatest of goods. You are right, he said. Once again I reconsidered and said:

greek_head: ἐγενόμεθα ὑπὸ τῶν ξένων ἐγώ τε καὶ σύ ὦ παῖ Ἀξιόχου Τί δή ἔφη τοῦτο Ὅτι εὐτυχίαν ἐν τοῖς ἔμπροσθεν
greek_tokens: gignomai upo o cenos egw te kai su eimi pais acioxos tis dh fhmi outos ostis eutuxia en o emprosqen tiqhmi nundi auqis peri o autos legw tis oun dh outos katagelastos dhpou o pala prokeimai outos palin protiqhmi kai dis tautos legw pws fhmi outos legw o sofia dhpou eimi de egw eutuxia eimi outos de kan pas gignwskw kai os qaumazw outws eti neos te kai euhqhs eimi agos gignwskw autos qaumazw ara
english_head: We have almost made ourselves laughing-stocks, you and I, son of Axiochus, for our visitors. What is wrong now? he asked. Why, after putting good fortune in our former list, we have just been discussing the same thing again. What is the point? Surely it is ridiculous, when a thing has been before us all the time, to set it forth again and go over the same ground twice. To what are you referring? he asked. Wisdom, I replied, is presumably good fortune: even a child could see that. He wondered at this—he is still so young and simple-minded: then I, perceiving his surprise, went on: Can you be unaware,

greek_head: οὐκ οἶσθα ἔφην ὦ Κλεινία ὅτι περὶ αὐλημάτων εὐπραγίαν οἱ αὐληταὶ εὐτυχέστατοί εἰσιν Συνέφη Οὐκοῦν ἦν δ'
greek_tokens: ou oida fhmi eimi kleinia ostis peri aulhma eupragia e aulhths eutuxhs eimi sumfhmi oukoun eimi de egw kai peri gramma grafeus te kai anagnwsis e grammatisths panu ge tis de pros o o qalassa kindunos mhs oiomai eutuxhs tis eimi o sofos kubernhths ws epi pas eipon ou dhta tis de strateuw meta poteros an hdus o kindunos te kai o
english_head: Cleinias, that for success in flute-music it is the flute-players that have the best fortune? He agreed to this. Then in writing and reading letters it will be the schoolmasters. Certainly. Well now, for the dangers of a sea-voyage, do you consider any pilots to he more fortunate, as a general rule, than the wise ones? No, to be sure. Well, then, suppose you were on a campaign, with which kind of general would you prefer to share both the peril and the luck—a wise one, or an ignorant? With a wise one. Well then, supposing you were sick, with which kind of doctor would you like to venture yourself a wise one, or an ignorant? With a wise one.

We came to an agreement somehow or other

Then can we, in Heaven’s name, get any benefit from all the other possessions without understanding and wisdom? Shall we say that a man will profit more by possessing much and doing much when he has no sense, than he will if he does and possesses little? Consider it this way:

english_head: He agreed. Let us consider then, I said, the further conclusion that lies before us. Since we are all eager to be happy, and since we were found to become so by not only using things but using them aright, while knowledge, we saw, was that which provided the rightness and good fortune, it seems that every man must prepare himself by all available means so that he may be as wise as possible. Is it not so? Yes, he said. And if a man thinks, as well he may,

english_head: they also make? Yes. Now, is speaking doing and making? He agreed that it is. No one, I suppose, speaks what is not—for thereby he would be making something; and you have agreed that one cannot so much as make what is not—so that, by your account, no one speaks what is false, while if Dionysodorus speaks, he speaks what is true and is. Yes, in faith, Euthydemus, said Ctesippus; but somehow or other he speaks what is, only not as it is. How do you mean, Ctesippus? said Dionysodorus.

Well, he went on, good things are in good case, bad in bad, are they not? He assented. And you admit that gentlemen tell things as they are. I do. Then, Ctesippus, good people speak evil of evil things, if they speak of them as they are. Yes, I can tell you, very much so, when for instance they speak of evil men; among whom, if you take my advice,

If you young fellows are afraid, let the experiment be made on me

Yes, he said, for if you recollect, Ctesippus, we showed just now that no one speaks of a thing as it is not; since we saw that no one speaks what is not. Well, what of that? asked Ctesippus: are you and I contradicting any the less? Now tell me, he said, could we contradict if we both spoke the description of the same thing? In this case should we not surely speak the same words? He agreed. But when neither of us speaks the description of the thing, he asked,

Well now, when I for my part speak the description of the thing, while you give another of another thing, do we contradict then? Or do I describe the thing, while you do not describe it at all? How can he who does not describe contradict him who does? At this Ctesippus was silent; but I, wondering at the argument, said: How do you mean, Dionysodorus?

english_head: For, to be plain with you, this argument, though I have heard it from many people on various occasions, never fails to set me wondering—you know the followers of Protagoras made great use of it, as did others even before his time, but to me it always seems to have a wonderful way of upsetting not merely other views but itself also—and I believe I shall learn the truth of it from you far better than from anyone else. There is no such thing as speaking false—that is the substance of your statement, is it not? Either one must speak and speak the truth, or else not speak? He agreed.

english_head: Then shall we say that speaking false is not, but thinking false is? No, it is the same with thinking, he said. So neither is there any false opinion, I said, at all. No, he said. Nor ignorance, nor ignorant men; or must not ignorance occur, if it ever can, when we put things falsely? Certainly, he said. But there is no such thing as this, I said. No, he said. Is it merely to save your statement, Dionysodorus, that you state it so—just to say something startling—or is it really and truly your view that there is no such thing as an ignorant man?

Well, Euthydemus, I said, it is because I do not at all understand these clever devices and palpable hits: I am only a dull sort of thinker. Well, you see, I replied, they are so very hard, and naturally so; for they fall from the lips of wise men; and this is further shown by the extreme difficulty of dealing with this last one you put forward. Only they are unwilling to give us a display of it in real earnest, but treat us to jugglers’ tricks in the style of Proteus the Egyptian adept.

english_head: So let us take our cue from Menelaus, and not leave hold of these gentlemen till they give us a sight of their own serious business. I believe something very fine will be found in them as soon as they begin to be serious. Come, let us beg and exhort and beseech them to let their light shine. For my part, then, I am minded to take the lead once more in showing what sort of persons I pray may be revealed in them:

english_head: starting from where I left off before, I shall try, as best I can, to describe what follows on from that, to see if I can rouse them to action and make them, in merciful commensuration of my earnest endeavor, be earnest themselves. Will you, Cleinias, I asked, please remind me of the point at which we left off? Now, as far as I can tell, it was something like this: we ended by agreeing that one ought to pursue wisdom, did we not? Yes, he said. And this pursuit—called philosophy—is an acquiring of knowledge. Is it not so? I asked. Yes, he said. Then what knowledge should we acquire if we acquired it rightly?

greek_head: κτησαίμεθα ἆρ' οὐ τοῦτο μὲν ἁπλοῦν ὅτι ταύτην ἥτις ἡμᾶς ὀνήσει Πάνυ γ' ἔφη Ἆρ' οὖν ἄν τι ἡμᾶς ὀνήσειεν εἰ

english_head: Is it not absolutely clear that it must be that knowledge which will profit us? Certainly, he said. Now will it profit us at all, if we know how to tell, as we go about, where the earth has most gold buried in it? Perhaps, he said. But yet, I went on, we refuted that former proposition, agreeing that even if without any trouble or digging the earth we got all the gold in the world, we should gain nothing, so that not if we knew how

english_head: to turn the rocks into gold would our knowledge be of any worth. For unless we know how to use the gold, we found no advantage in it. Do you not remember? I asked. Certainly I do, he said. Nor, it seems, do we get any advantage from all other knowledge, whether of money-making or medicine or any other that knows how to make things, without knowing how to use the thing made. Is it not so? He agreed. Nor again, if there is a knowledge

english_head: enabling one to make men immortal, does this, if we lack the knowledge how to use immortality, seem to bring any advantage either, if we are to infer anything from our previous admissions. On all these points we agreed. Then the sort of knowledge we require, fair youth, I said, is that in which there happens to be a union of making and knowing how to use the thing made. Apparently, he said. So we ought, it seems, to aim at something far other than being lyre-makers

english_head: or possessing that kind of knowledge. For in this case the art that makes and the art that uses are quite distinct, dealing in separation with the same thing; since there is a wide difference between the art of making lyres and that of harp-playing. Is it not so? He agreed. Nor again, obviously, do we require an art of flute-making; for this is another of the same kind. He assented. Now in good earnest, I asked, if we were to learn the art of speech-making, can that be the art we should acquire if we would be happy? I for one think not, said Cleinias, interposing.

greek_head: Τίνι τεκμηρίῳ ἦν δ' ἐγώ χρῇ Ὁρῶ ἔφη τινὰς λογοποιούς οἳ τοῖς ἰδίοις λόγοις οἷς

english_head: On what proof do you rely? I asked. I see, he said, certain speech-writers who do not know how to use the special arguments composed by themselves, just as lyre-makers in regard to their lyres: in the former case also there are other persons able to use what the makers produced, while being themselves unable to make the written speech. Hence it is clear that in speech likewise there are two distinct arts, one of making and one of using. I think you give sufficient proof, I said, that this art of the speech-writers cannot be that whose acquisition would make one happy. And yet I fancied that somewhere about this point would appear the knowledge which we have been seeking all this while.

greek_head: τὴν ἐπιστήμην ἣν δὴ πάλαι ζητοῦμεν καὶ γάρ μοι οἵ τε ἄνδρες αὐτοὶ οἱ λογοποιοί ὅταν συγγένωμαι αὐτοῖς ὑπέρσοφοι

english_head: For not only do these speech-writers themselves, when I am in their company, impress me as prodigiously clever, Cleinias, but their art itself seems so exalted as to be almost inspired. However, this is not surprising; for it is a part of the sorcerer’s art,

greek_head: ἡ μὲν γὰρ τῶν ἐπῳδῶν ἔχεών τε καὶ φαλαγγίων καὶ σκορπίων καὶ τῶν ἄλλων θηρίων τε καὶ νόσων κήλησίς

english_head: and only slightly inferior to that. The sorcerer’s art is the charming of snakes and tarantulas and scorpions and other beasts and diseases, while the other is just the charming and soothing of juries, assemblies, crowds, and so forth. Or does it strike you differently? I asked. No, it appears to me, he replied, to be as you say. Which way then, said I, shall we turn now? What kind of art shall we try? For my part, he said, I have no suggestion. Why, I think I have found it myself, I said. What is it? said Cleinias.

greek_head: Ἡ στρατηγική μοι δοκεῖ ἔφην ἐγώ τέχνη παντὸς μᾶλλον εἶναι ἣν ἄν τις κτησάμενος εὐδαίμων εἴη

english_head: Generalship, I replied, strikes me as the art whose acquisition above all others would make one happy. I do not think so. Why not? I asked. In a sense, this is an art of hunting men. What then? I said. No part of actual hunting, he replied, covers more than the province of chasing and overcoming; and when they have overcome the creature they are chasing, they are unable to use it: the huntsmen or the fishermen hand it over to the caterers, and so it is too with the geometers, astronomers, and calculators—

greek_head: δ' αὖ γεωμέτραι καὶ οἱ ἀστρονόμοι καὶ οἱ λογιστικοί θηρευτικοὶ γάρ εἰσι καὶ οὗτοι οὐ γὰρ ποιοῦσι τὰ διαγράμματα

english_head: for these also are hunters in their way, since they are not in each case diagram-makers, but discover the realities of things—and so, not knowing how to use their prey, but only how to hunt, I take it they hand over their discoveries to the dialecticians to use properly, those of them, at least, who are not utter blockheads. Very good, I said, most handsome and ingenious Cleinias; and is this really so? To be sure it is; and so, in the same way, with the generals. When they have hunted either a city or

english_head: an army, they hand it over to the politicians—since they themselves do not know how to use what they have hunted—just as quail-hunters, I suppose, hand over their birds to the quail-keepers. If, therefore, he went on, we are looking for that art which itself shall know how to use what it has acquired either in making or chasing, and if this is the sort that will make us blest, we must reject generalship, he said, and seek out some other.

english_head: What is this, Socrates? Such a pronouncement from that stripling! You do not believe it is his, Crito? I should rather think not. For I am sure, if he spoke thus, he has no need of education from Euthydemus or anyone else. But then, Heaven help me! I wonder if it was Ctesippus who said it, and my memory fails me.

Tell me, mysterious Crito, was it some superior power that was there to speak it? For that speech I heard, I am sure. Yes, I promise you, Socrates: I fancy it was indeed some superior power—very much so. But after that, did you go on looking for a suitable art? Did you find the one which you had as the object of your search, or not?

english_head: Find it, my good fellow! No, we were in a most ridiculous state; like children who run after crested larks, we kept on believing each moment we were just going to catch this or that one of the knowledges, while they as often slipped from our grasp. What need to tell you the story at length? When we reached the kingly art, and were examining it to see if we had here what provides and produces happiness, at this point we were involved in a labyrinth: when we supposed we had arrived at the end, we twisted about again

We took the view that the statesman’s and the monarch’s arts were one and the same. Well, what then? To this art, we thought, generalship and the other arts handed over the management of the productions of their own trades, as this one alone knew how to use them. So it seemed clear to us that this was the one we were seeking,

And what about your art of agriculture? In ruling over all

In ruling over all that comes under its rule, what does it produce? Perhaps you are not quite ready with the answer. I am not indeed, Socrates. Nor were we, Crito; yet so much you know, that if this is really the one we are seeking, it must be beneficial. Certainly. Then surely it must purvey something good? Necessarily, Socrates.

greek_head: Ἀγαθὸν δέ γέ που ὡμολογήσαμεν ἀλλήλοις ἐγώ τε καὶ Κλεινίας οὐδὲν εἶναι ἄλλο ἢ ἐπιστήμην τινά

english_head: And you know we agreed with each other, Cleinias and I, that nothing can be good but some sort of knowledge. Yes, so you told me. And it was found that all effects in general that you may ascribe to statesmanship—and a great many of them there must be, presumably, if the citizens are to be made wealthy and free and immune from faction—all these things were neither bad nor good, while this art must make us wise and impart knowledge, if it really was to be the one which benefited us

greek_head: ὠφελοῦσά τε καὶ εὐδαίμονας ποιοῦσα Ἔστι ταῦτα τότε γοῦν οὕτως ὑμῖν ὡμολογήθη ὡς

english_head: and made us happy. True: so at all events you agreed then, by your account of the discussion. Then do you think that kingship makes men wise and good? Why not, Socrates? But does it make all men good, and in all things? And is this the art that confers every sort of knowledge—shoe-making and carpentry and so forth? No, I think not, Socrates.

greek_head: Ἀλλὰ τίνα δὴ ἐπιστήμην ᾗ τί χρησόμεθα τῶν μὲν γὰρ ἔργων οὐδενὸς δεῖ αὐτὴν δημιουργὸν εἶναι τῶν μήτε

english_head: Well, what knowledge does it give ? What use can we make of it? It is not to be a producer of any of the effects which are neither bad nor good, while it is to confer no other knowledge but itself. Shall we try and say what it is, and what use we shall make of it? Do you mind if we describe it, Crito, as that whereby we shall make other men good? I quite agree. And in what respect are we going to have these men good, and in what useful? Or shall we venture to say they are to make others so, and these again others? In what respect they can possibly be good is nowhere evident to us,

greek_head: ὅτι δέ ποτε ἀγαθοί εἰσιν οὐδαμοῦ ἡμῖν φαίνονται ἐπειδήπερ τὰ ἔργα τὰ λεγόμενα εἶναι τῆς πολιτικῆς ἠτιμάσαμεν ἀλλ'

english_head: since we have discredited all the business commonly called politics, and it is merely a case of the proverbial Corinthus Divine; and, as I was saying, we are equally or even worse at fault as to what that knowledge can be which is to make us happy. Upon my word, Socrates, you got yourselves there, it seems, into a pretty fix. So then I myself, Crito, finding
