english_head: I had fallen into this perplexity, began to exclaim at the top of my voice, beseeching the two strangers as though I were calling upon the Heavenly Twins to save us, the lad and myself, from the mighty wave of the argument, and to give us the best of their efforts, and this done, to make plain to us what that knowledge can be of which we must get hold if we are to spend the remainder of our lives in a proper way Well, did Euthydemus consent to propound anything for you? Why, certainly; and he began his discourse, my good friend, in this very lofty-minded fashion:

greek_head: Πότερον δή σε ἔφη ὦ Σώκρατες ταύτην τὴν ἐπιστήμην περὶ ἣν πάλαι ἀπορεῖτε διδάξω ἢ ἐπιδείξω ἔχοντα
greek_tokens: poteros dh su fhmi eimi swkraths outos o episthmh peri os pala aforaw didaskw h epideiknumi exw eimi makarios eimi de egw eimi de epi sos outos panu men oun fhmi epideiknumi toinun egw nh diw fhmi egw exw polus gar raios h manqanw thlikosde anhr ferw dh egw apokrinw fhmi eimi ostis efisthmi panu ge eimi de egw kai polus mikros ge arkew fhmi ara oun dokew oios su tis o eimi outos o tugxanw eimi autos outos
english_head: Would you rather, Socrates, that I instructed you as to this knowledge which has baffled you all this while, or propound that you have it? O gifted sir, I exclaimed, and have you the power to do this? Certainly I have, he replied. Then for Heaven’s sake, I cried, propound that I have it! This will be much easier than learning foraman of my age. Come then, answer me this, he said: Do you know anything? Yes, indeed, I replied. and many things, though trifles. That is enough, he said; now do you think it possible that anything that is should not be just that which it actually is? On my soul, not I.

greek_head: μὴ εἶναι Ἀλλὰ μὰ Δί' οὐκ ἔγωγε Οὐκοῦν σύ ἔφη ἐπίστασαί τι Ἔγωγε Οὐκοῦν ἐπιστήμων εἶ εἴπερ ἐπίστασαι
greek_tokens: mh eimi alla ma diw ou egw oukoun su fhmi efisthmi tis egw oukoun episthmos ei eiper efisthmi panu ge outos ge autos oudeis diaferw alla ou anagkh su exis pas efisthmi episthmwn ge eimi ma diw fhmi egw epei polus allos ou efisthmi oukoun ei tis mh efisthmi ou episthmos ei ekeinos ge eimi filos eimi de egw hsswn oun tis fhmi ou episthmos ei arti de episthmos fhmi eimi kai outws tugxanw eimi
english_head: Now you, he said, know something? I do. Then you are knowing, if you really know? Certainly, in just that something. That makes no difference; you are not under a necessity of knowing everything, if you are knowing? No, to be sure, I replied; for there are many other things which I do not know. Then if you do not know something, you are not knowing? Not in that thing, my dear sir, I replied. Are you therefore any the less unknowing? Just now you said you were knowing;

greek_head: αὐτὸς οὗτος ὃς εἶ καὶ αὖ πάλιν οὐκ εἶ κατὰ ταὐτὰ ἅμα Εἶεν ἦν δ' ἐγώ Εὐθύδημε τὸ γὰρ λεγόμενον καλὰ
greek_tokens: autos outos os ei kai au palin ou ei kata tautos ama eien eimi de egw euqudhmos o gar legw kalos dh patagew pws oun efisthmi ekeinos o episthmh os zhtew ws dh outos adunatos eimi o autos eimi te kai mh eiper eis efisthmi apas efisthmi ou gar an eimi episthmos te kai anepisthmwn ama epei de pas efisthmi ekeinos dh o episthmh exw ara outws legw kai outos eimi o sofos
english_head: so here you are, actually the very man that you are, and again, not that man, in regard to the same matter and at the same time! Admitted, Euthydemus, I said: as the saying goes, well said whate’er you say. How therefore do I know that knowledge which we were seeking? Since forsooth it is impossible for the same thing to be so and not be so; by knowing one thing I know all;—for I could not be at once both knowing and unknowing;—and as I know everything I have that knowledge to boot: is that your line of argument? Is this your wisdom?

greek_head: Αὐτὸς σαυτόν γε δὴ ἐξελέγχεις ἔφη ὦ Σώκρατες Τί δέ ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὦ Εὐθύδημε σὺ οὐ πέπονθας τοῦτο τὸ
greek_tokens: autos sautou ge dh ecelegxw fhmi eimi swkraths tis de eimi de egw eimi euqudhmos su ou pasxw outos o autos paqos egw gar su meta sew otioun an pasxw kai meta dionusodwros ode filos kefalh ou an panu aganaktew eipon egw sfeis ou o men efisthmi o eimi o de ou efisthmi hkistos ge fhmi eimi swkraths o dionusodwros pws legw fhmi egw alla oudeis ara efisthmi kai mala eimi de os
english_head: Yes, you see, Socrates, he said, your own words refute you. Well, but, Euthydemus, I continued, are you not in the same plight? I assure you, so long as I had you and this dear fellow Dionysodorus to share my lot, however hard, I should have nothing to complain of. Tell me, you both know some existent things, of course, and others you do not? By no means, Socrates, said Dionysodorus. How do you mean? I asked: do you then not know anything? Oh yes, we do, he said.

greek_head: Πάντ' ἄρα ἔφην ἐγώ ἐπίστασθον ἐπειδήπερ καὶ ὁτιοῦν Πάντ' ἔφη καὶ σύ γε πρός εἴπερ καὶ ἓν ἐπίστασαι πάντα
greek_tokens: panth ara fhmi egw efisthmi epei kai otioun panth fhmi kai su ge pros eiper kai eis efisthmi pas efisthmi eimi zew fhmi egw ws qaumastos legw kai agaqos megas fainw mhs kai e allos pas anqrwpos panth efisthmi h oudeis ou gar dhpou fhmi o men efisthmi o de ou efisthmi kai eimi ama episthmwn te kai anepisthmwn alla tis eimi de egw pas eimi de os pas efisthmi eiper kai eis
english_head: So you know everything, I asked, since you know anything? Everything, he replied; yes, and you too, if you know one thing, know all. Good Heavens, I cried, what a wonderful statement! What a great blessing to boast of! And the rest of mankind, do they know everything or nothing? Surely, he said, they cannot know some things and not others, and so be at once knowing and unknowing. But what then? I asked. All men, he replied, know all things, if they know one. In the name of goodness,

greek_head: Ὦ πρὸς τῶν θεῶν ἦν δ' ἐγώ ὦ Διονυσόδωρε δῆλοι γάρ μοί ἐστον ἤδη ὅτι σπουδάζετον καὶ μόλις ὑμᾶς προυκαλεσάμην
greek_tokens: eimi pros o qea eimi de egw eimi dionusodwros dhlos gar egw eimi hdh ostis spoudazw kai molis su prokalew spoudazw autos o eimi pas efisthmi oios tektonikos kai skutikos panu ge fhmi eimi kai neurorrafew dunatos eimi kai nai ma dios kassuw fhmi eimi kai o toioutos o asthr oposos eimi kai o amos panu ge eimi de os eimi ou an oiomai omologew egw kai o kthsippos upolambanw pros zeus fhmi dionusodwros
english_head: Dionysodorus, I said—for now I can see both of you are serious; before, I could hardly prevail on you to be so—do you yourselves really know everything? Carpentry, for instance, and shoe-making? Certainly, he said. And you are good hands at leather-stitching? Why yes, in faith, and cobbling, he said. And are you good also at such things as counting the stars, and the sand? Certainly, he said: can you think we would not admit that also? Here Ctesippus broke in: Be so good,

greek_head: τεκμήριόν τί μοι τούτων ἐπιδείξατον τοιόνδε ᾧ εἴσομαι ὅτι ἀληθῆ λέγετον
greek_tokens: tekmhrion tis egw outos epideiknumi toiosde os eimi ostis alhqhs legw tis epideiknumi fhmi oida euqudhmos oposos odous exis kai o euqudhmos oposos su ou ecarkew su fhmi akew ostis pas efisthmi mhdamos eimi de os alla outos eti egw monos eipon kai epideiknumi ostis alhqhs legw kai ean eipon oposos ekateros exis su kai fainw gignwskw egw ariqmew hdh pasxw su kai allos
english_head: Dionysodorus, he said, as to place some such evidence before me as will convince me that what you say is true. What shall I put forward? he asked. Do you know how many teeth Euthydemus has, and does Euthydemus know how many you have? Are you not content, he rejoined, to be told that we know everything? No, do not say that, he replied: only tell us this one thing more, and propound to us that you speak the truth. Then, if you tell us how many teeth each of you has, and you are found by our counting to have known it, we shall believe you thenceforth in everything else likewise.

greek_head: Ἡγουμένω οὖν σκώπτεσθαι οὐκ ἠθελέτην ἀλλ' ὡμολογησάτην πάντα χρήματα ἐπίστασθαι καθ' ἓν ἕκαστον
greek_tokens: hgeomai oun skwptw ou eqelw alla omologew pas xrhma efisthmi kaqa eis ekastos erwtaw upo kthsippos o gar kthsippos panu aparakaluptos oudeis ostis ou erwtaw teleutaw kai o aisxros ei efisthmi o de andreios omose eimi o erwthma omologew oida wsper e kapros e pros o plhgh omose wqew wste egw kai autos eimi kritwn upo apistia anagkazw teleutaw eromai o euqudhmos ei
english_head: Well, as they supposed we were making fun of them, they would not do it: only they agreed that they knew all subjects, when questioned on them, one after the other, by Ctesippus; who, before he had done with them, asked them if they knew every kind of thing, even the most unseemly, without the least reserve; while they most valiantly encountered his questions, agreeing that they had the knowledge in each case, like boars when driven up to face the spears: so that I for my part, Crito, became quite incredulous,and had to ask in the end if Dionysodorus knew

greek_head: καὶ ὀρχεῖσθαι ἐπίσταιτο ὁ Διονυσόδωρος ὁ δέ Πάνυ ἔφη Οὐ δήπου ἦν δ' ἐγώ καὶ ἐς μαχαίρας γε κυβιστᾶν καὶ
greek_tokens: kai orxeomai efisthmi o dionusodwros o de panu fhmi ou dhpou eimi de egw kai eis maxaira ge kubistaw kai epi troxos dinew thlikoutos eimi outws prosw sofia hkw oudeis fhmi ostis ou poteros de eimi de egw pas nun monos efisthmi h kai aei kai aei fhmi kai oste paidia eimi kai euqus gignomai epistamai pas fhmi ama amfoteros
english_head: also how to dance. To which he replied: Certainly. I do not suppose, I said, that you have attained such a degree of skill as to do sword-dancing, or be whirled about on a wheel, at your time of life? There is nothing, he said, that I cannot do. Then tell me, I went on, do you know everything at present only, or for ever? For ever too, he said. And when you were children, and were just born, you knew? Everything, they both replied together.

greek_head: Καὶ ἡμῖν μὲν ἄπιστον ἐδόκει τὸ πρᾶγμα εἶναι ὁ δ' Εὐθύδημος Ἀπιστεῖς ἔφη ὦ Σώκρατες
greek_tokens: kai egw men apistos dokew o pragma eimi o de euqudhmos apistew fhmi eimi swkraths plhn ge ostis eimi de egw eoika su eimi sofos eimi alla ean fhmi eqelw egw apokrinw egw epideiknumi kai sos outos o qaumasths omologew alla meis eimi de egw hdus outos ecelegxw ei gar su lanqanw emautou sofos eimi su de outos epideicis ws pas efisthmi kai aei tis megas ermaion autos an euriskw en pas o bios apokrinw dh fhmi
english_head: Now, to us the thing seemed incredible: then Euthydemus said: You do not believe it, Socrates? I will only say, I replied, that you must indeed be clever. Why, he said, if you will consent to answer me, I will propound that you too admit these surprising facts. Oh, I am only too glad, I replied, to be refuted in the matter. For if I am not aware of my own cleverness, and you are going to show me that I know everything always, what greater stroke of luck than this could befall me in all my living days? Then answer me, he said. Ask: I am ready to answer.

greek_head: Ὡς ἀποκρινουμένου ἐρώτα Ἆρ' οὖν ἔφη ὦ Σώκρατες ἐπιστήμων του εἶ ἢ οὔ
greek_tokens: ws apokrinw erwtaw ara oun fhmi eimi swkraths episthmos tis ei h ou egw poteros oun os episthmos ei outos kai efisthmi h allos tis os episthmos oimh gar su o yuxh legw h ou outos legw ou aisxunh fhmi eimi swkraths erwtaw anterwtaw eien eimi de egw alla pws poiew outws gar poiew opws an su keleuw otan mh oida ostis erwtaw keleuw egw omws apokrinw alla mh epaneromai
english_head: Well then, Socrates, he asked, have you knowledge of something, or not? I have. And tell me, do you know with that whereby you have knowledge, or with something else? With that whereby I have knowledge: I think you mean the soul, or is not that your meaning? Are you not ashamed, Socrates, he said, to ask a question on your side when you are being questioned? Very well, I said: but how am I to proceed? I will do just as you bid me. When I cannot tell what you are asking, is it your order that I answer all the same, without asking a question upon it? Why, he replied, you surely conceive

greek_head: Ὑπολαμβάνεις γὰρ δήπου τι ἔφη ὃ λέγω Ἔγωγε ἦν δ' ἐγώ
greek_tokens: upolambanw gar dhpou tis fhmi o legos egw eimi de egw pros outos toinun apokrinw o upolambanw tis oun fhmi an su men allh erwtaw dianoeomai egw de allh upolambanw epeita pros outos apokrinw ecarkew su ean mhdeis pros epos apokrinw egw eimi de os ou mentoi sos ge ws oimh ou toinun ma dios apokrinw eimi de egw proteros prin an puqw ou apokrinw fhmi pros o an aei upolambanw ostis exw fluarew kai arxaios ei o deon
english_head: some meaning in what I say? I do, I replied. Answer then to the meaning you conceive to be in my words. Well, I said, if you ask a question with a different meaning in your mind from that which I conceive, and I answer to the latter, are you content I should answer nothing to the point? For my part, he replied, I shall be content: you, however, will not, so far as I can see. Then I declare I shall not answer, I said, before I get it right. You refuse to answer, he said, to the meaning you conceive in each case,

greek_head: Κἀγὼ ἔγνων αὐτὸν ὅτι μοι χαλεπαίνοι διαστέλλοντι τὰ λεγόμενα βουλόμενός με θηρεῦσαι τὰ ὀνόματα περιστήσας
greek_tokens: agos gignwskw autos ostis egw xalepainw diastellw o legw boulomai egw qhraw o onoma periisthmi anamimnhskw oun o konnos ostis egw ekeinos xalepainw ekastote otan autos mh ufihmi epeita egw hsswn epimeleomai ws amaqhs eimi epei de oun dianoeomai kai para outos foiths oiomai dew ufihmi mh egw skaios hgeomai foithths mh prosdexomai eipon oun alla ei dokew su
english_head: because you will go on driveling, you hopeless old dotard! Here I perceived he was annoyed with me for distinguishing between the phrases used, when he wanted to entrap me in his verbal snares. So I remembered Connus, how he too is annoyed with me whenever I do not give in to him, with the result that he now takes less trouble over me as being a stupid person. So being minded to take lessons from this new teacher, I decided that I had better give in, lest he should take me for a blockhead and not admit me to his classes. So I said: Well, if you think fit, Euthydemus,

greek_head: Εὐθύδημε οὕτω ποιεῖν ποιητέον σὺ γὰρ πάντως που κάλλιον ἐπίστασαι διαλέγεσθαι ἢ ἐγώ τέχνην ἔχων ἰδιώτου
greek_tokens: euqudhmos outws poiew poihteos su gar pantws pou kallion efisthmi dialegw h egw texnh exw idiwths anqrwpos erwtaw oun palin ek arxh apokrinw dh fhmi palin poteros efisthmi tis o efisthmi h ou egw fhmi o ge yuxw
english_head: to proceed thus, we must do so; in any case I suppose you understand debating better than I do—you are versed in the method, and I am but a layman. Begin your questions, then, over again. Now, answer me once more, he said: do you know what you know by means of something, or not? I do, I replied; by means of my soul.

greek_head: Οὗτος αὖ ἔφη προσαποκρίνεται τοῖς ἐρωτωμένοις οὐ γὰρ ἔγωγε ἐρωτῶ ὅτῳ ἀλλ' εἰ ἐπίστασαί τῳ
greek_tokens: outos au fhmi prosapokrinomai o erwtaw ou gar egw erwtaw ostis alla ei efisthmi tis pleos au fhmi egw o deon apokrinw upo apaideusia alla suggignwskw egw apokrinw gar hdh aplos ostis efisthmi tis o efisthmi poteros eimi de os o autos outos ge aei h eimi men oste outos eimi de oste eteros aei otan epistamai eimi de egw outos ou au fhmi pauw parafqeggomai alla opws mh tis egw sfallw o aei outos
english_head: There he is again, he said, answering more than he is asked. For I am not asking what the means is, but only whether you know by some means. Yes, I did again answer more than I ought, I said, through lack of education. But forgive me, and I will now simply reply that I know what I know by some means. By one and the same means always, he asked, or sometimes by one and sometimes by another? Always, whenever I know, I replied, it is by this means. There again, he cried, you really must stop adding these qualifications.

greek_head: Οὔκουν ἡμᾶς γ' ἔφη ἀλλ' εἴπερ σέ ἀλλ' ἀποκρίνου ἦ ἀεὶ τούτῳ ἐπίστασαι Ἀεί ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἐπειδὴ δεῖ ἀφελεῖν
greek_tokens: oukoun egw ge fhmi alla eiper sos alla apokrinw eimi aei outos efisthmi aei eimi de egw epei deomai afairew o otan oukoun aei men outos efisthmi aei de efisthmi poteros o men outos efisthmi os efisthmi o de allos h outos pas outos fhmi egw apas o ge efisthmi outos ekeinos fhmi hkw o autos parafqegma alla afairew fhmi egw o o ge efisthmi alla mhde eis fhmi afairew oudeis gar su deomai
english_head: But I am so afraid this word always may bring us to grief. Not us, he rejoined, but, if anyone, you. Now answer: do you know by this means always? Always, I, replied, since I must withdraw the whenever. Then you always know by this means: that being the case, do you know some things by this means of knowing, and some things by another means, or everything by this? Everything by this, I replied; everything, that is, that I know. There it comes again, he cried; the same qualification! Well, I withdraw my that is, that I know. No, do not withdraw a single word, he said: I ask you for no concession.

greek_head: ἀλλά μοι ἀπόκριναι δύναιο ἂν ἅπαντα ἐπίστασθαι εἰ μὴ πάντα ἐπίσταιο
greek_tokens: alla egw apokrinw dunamai an apas efisthmi ei mh pas epistamai teras gar an eaw eimi de egw kai os eipon prostiqhmi toinun hdh ostis boulomai apas gar omologew efisthmi eoika fhmi egw epei ge oudeis exis dunamis o o efisthmi pas de efisthmi oukoun kai aei omologew efisthmi outos os efisthmi eite otan epistamai eite opws boulomai aei gar omologew efisthmi kai ama pas dhlos oun ostis kai pas eimi
english_head: Only answer me: could you know all things if you did not know everything? It would be most surprising, I said. Then he went on: You may therefore add on now whatever you please: for you admit that you know all things. It seems I do, I replied, seeing that my that I know has no force, and I know everything. Now you have also admitted that you know always by the means whereby you know, whenever you know—or however you like to put it. For you have admitted that you always know and, at the same time, everything. Hence it is clear that

greek_head: ἠπίστω καὶ ὅτ' ἐγίγνου καὶ ὅτ' ἐφύου καὶ πρὶν αὐτὸς γενέσθαι καὶ πρὶν οὐρανὸν καὶ γῆν γενέσθαι ἠπίστω
greek_tokens: epistamai kai oste gignomai kai oste efuw kai prin autos gignomai kai prin ouranos kai gaia gignomai epistamai apas eiper aei efisthmi kai nai ma dios fhmi autos aei efisthmi kai apas an egw boulomai alla boulomai eimi de egw eimi polutimhtos euqudhmos ei dh o eimi alhqhs legw alla ou su panu pisteuw ikanos eimi ei mh su sumboulomai o adelfos su outos dionusodwros outws de taxa an eipon de egw eimi de egw
english_head: even as a child you knew, both when you were being born and when you were being conceived: and before you yourself came into being or heaven and earth existed, you knew all things, since you always know. Yes, and I declare, he said, you yourself will always know all things, if it be my pleasure. Oh, pray let it be your pleasure, I replied, most worshipful Euthydemus, if what you say is really true. Only I do not quite trust in your efficacy, if your pleasure is not to he also that of your brother here, Dionysodorus: if it is, you will probably prevail. And tell me, I went on,

greek_head: τὰ μὲν γὰρ ἄλλα οὐκ ἔχω ὑμῖν πῶς ἀμφισβητοίην οὕτως εἰς σοφίαν τερατώδεσιν ἀνθρώποις ὅπως ἐγὼ οὐ πάντα
greek_tokens: o men gar allos ou exw su pws amfisbhtew outws eis sofia teratwdhs anqrwpos opws egw ou pas efisthmi epei ge su fhmi o de toiosde pws fhmi efisthmi euqudhmos ws e agaqos anhr adikos eimi ferw eipon outos efisthmi h ou efisthmi efisthmi mentoi fhmi tis eimi de egw ostis ou adikos eimi e agaqos
english_head: since I cannot hope in a general way to dispute the statement that I know everything with persons so prodigiously clever—since it is your statement—how am I to say I know certain things, Euthydemus; for instance, that good men are unjust? Come, tell me, do I know this or not? You know it certainly, he said. What? I said. That the good are not unjust. Quite so, I said: I knew that all the time; but that is not what I ask:

greek_head: Πάνυ γε ἦν δ' ἐγώ πάλαι ἀλλ' οὐ τοῦτο ἐρωτῶ ἀλλ' ὡς ἄδικοί εἰσιν οἱ ἀγαθοί ποῦ ἐγὼ τοῦτο ἔμαθον
greek_tokens: panu ge eimi de egw pala alla ou outos erwtaw alla ws adikos eimi e agaqos pou egw outos manqanw oudamos fhmi o dionusodwros ou ara efisthmi fhmi outos egw diafqeirw fhmi o logos o euqudhmos pros o dionusodwros kai fainw outos ou efisthmi kai episthmos ama eimi kai anepisthmwn kai o dionusodwros eruqriaw alla su eimi de egw pws legw eimi euqudhmos ou dokew
english_head: tell me, where did I learn that the good are unjust? Nowhere, said Dionysodorus. Then I do not know this, I said. You are spoiling the argument, said Euthydemus to Dionysodorus, and we shall find that this fellow does not know, and is at once both knowing and unknowing. At this Dionysodorus reddened. But you, I said, what do you mean, Euthydemus.

greek_head: σοι ὀρθῶς ἁδελφὸς λέγειν ὁ πάντ' εἰδώς Ἀδελφὸς γάρ ἔφη ἐγώ εἰμι Εὐθυδήμου ταχὺ ὑπολαβὼν
greek_tokens: su orqos adelfos legw o panth eidoi adelfos gar fhmi egw eimi euqudhmos taxus upolambanw o dionusodwros agos eipon eaw agaqos ews an euqudhmos egw didacis ws efisthmi o agaqos anhr ostis adikos eimi kai mh egw fqonew o maqhma feugw fhmi eimi swkraths o dionusodwros kai ou eqelw apokrinw eoikotws ge eipon egw hsswn gar eimi kai o eteros su wste polus dew mh ou duo ge feugw polus gar
english_head: Do you find that your brother, who knows everything, has not spoken aright? I a brother of Euthydemus? quickly interposed Dionysodorus. Whereupon I said: Let me alone, good sir, till Euthydemus has taught me that I know that good men are unjust, and do not grudge me this lesson. You are running away, Socrates, said Dionysodorus; you refuse to answer. Yes, and with good reason, I said: for I am weaker than

greek_head: πού εἰμι φαυλότερος τοῦ Ἡρακλέους ὃς οὐχ οἷός τε ἦν τῇ τε ὕδρᾳ διαμάχεσθαι σοφιστρίᾳ οὔσῃ καὶ διὰ τὴν σοφίαν
greek_tokens: pou eimi faulos o hraklehs os ou oios te eimi o te udra diamaxomai sofistria eimi kai dia o sofia anihmi ei eis kefalh o logos tis apotemnw polus anti o eis kai karkinos tis eteros sofisths ek qalassa afizw newsti egw dokew kataplew os epei autos lupew outws ek o epi aristeros legos kai daknw o ioleus o adelfideos bohqos epikalew o de autos
english_head: either one of you, so I have no scruple about running away from the two together. You see, I am sadly inferior to Hercules, who was no match for the hydra—that she-professor who was so clever that she sent forth many heads of debate in place of each one that was cut off; nor for another sort of, crab-professor from the sea— freshly, I fancy, arrived on shore; and, when the hero was so bothered with its leftward barks and bites, he summoned his nephew Iolaus to the rescue,

greek_head: ἱκανῶς ἐβοήθησεν ὁ δ' ἐμὸς Ἰόλεως Πατροκλῆς εἰ ἔλθοι πλέον ἂν θάτερον ποιήσειεν

english_head: and he brought him effective relief. But if my Iolaus were to come, he would do more harm than good. Well, answer this, said Dionysodorus, now you have done your descanting: Was Iolaus more Hercules’ nephew than yours? I see I had best answer you, Dionysodorus, I said. For you will never cease putting questions—I think I may say I am sure of this—in a grudging, obstructing spirit, so that Euthydemus may not teach me that bit of cleverness. Then answer, he said. Well, I answer, I said, that Iolaus was Hercules’ nephew, but not mine,

greek_head: ἐμὸς δ' ὡς ἐμοὶ δοκεῖ οὐδ' ὁπωστιοῦν οὐ γὰρ Πατροκλῆς ἦν αὐτῷ πατήρ ὁ ἐμὸς ἀδελφός ἀλλὰ παραπλήσιον
greek_tokens: emos de ws egw dokew oude opwsoun ou gar patroklehs eimi autos pathr o emos adelfos alla paraplhsios men onoma ifiklehs o hraklehs adelfos patroklehs de eimi de os sos panu ge fhmi egw omomhtrios ge ou mentoi omopatrios adelfos ara eimi su kai ou adelfos ou omopatrios ge eimi beltistos fhmi ekeinos men gar xairedhmos eimi pathr emos de swfroniskos pathr de eimi fhmi swfroniskos kai xairedhmos panu
english_head: so far as I can see, in any way whatever. For Patrocles, my brother, was not his father; only Hercules’ brother Iphicles had a name somewhat similar to his. And Patrocles, he said, is your brother? Certainly, I said: that is, by the same mother, but not by the same father. Then he is your brother and not your brother. Not by the same father, worthy sir, I replied. His father was Chaeredemus, mine Sophroniscus. So Sophroniscus and Chaeredemus, he said, were father? Certainly, I said: the former mine,

english_head: the latter his. Then surely, he went on, Chaeredemus was other than father? Than mine, at any rate, I said. Why then, he was father while being other than father. Or are you the same as the stone? I fear you may prove that of me, I said, though I do not feel like it. Then are you other than the stone? Other, I must say. Then of course, he went on, if you are other than stone, you are not stone? And if you are other than gold, you are not gold? Quite so. Hence Chaeredemus, he said, being other than father,

greek_head: Εἰ γὰρ δήπου ἔφη πατήρ ἐστιν ὁ Χαιρέδημος ὑπολαβὼν ὁ Εὐθύδημος πάλιν αὖ ὁ Σωφρονίσκος ἕτερος ὢν πατρὸς

english_head: cannot be father. It seems, I said, that he is not a father. No, for I presume, interposed Euthydemus, that if Chaeredemus is a father Sophroniscus in his turn, being other than a father, is not a father; so that you, Socrates, are fatherless. Here Ctesippus took it up, observing: And your father too, is he not in just the same plight? Is he other than my father? Not in the slightest, said Euthydemus. What, asked the other, is he the same? The same, to be sure. I should not like to think he was: but tell me, Euthydemus,

english_head: is he my father only, or everybody else’s too? Everybody else’s too, he replied; or do you suppose that the same man, being a father, can be no father? I did suppose so, said Ctesippus. Well, said the other, and that a thing being gold could be not gold? Or being a man, not man? Perhaps, Euthydemus, said Ctesippus, you are knotting flax with cotton, as they say: for it is a strange result that you state, if your father is father of all. He is, though, was the reply. Of all men, do you mean? asked Ctesippus, or of horses too,

greek_head: Πάντων ἔφη Ἦ καὶ μήτηρ ἡ μήτηρ Καὶ ἡ μήτηρ γε Καὶ τῶν ἐχίνων ἄρα ἔφη ἡ σὴ μήτηρ μήτηρ ἐστὶ τῶν

english_head: and all other animals? Of all, he said. And is your mother a mother in the same way? My mother too. And is your mother a mother of sea-urchins? Yes, and yours is also, he replied. So then you are a brother of the gudgeons and whelps and porkers. Yes, and so are you, he said. Then your father is a boar and a dog. And so is yours, he said. Yes, said Dionysodorus, and it will take you but a moment, if you will answer me, Ctesippus, to acknowledge all this. Just tell me, have you a dog? Yes, a real rogue, said Ctesippus.

greek_head: αὐτῷ κυνίδια Καὶ μάλ' ἔφη ἕτερα τοιαῦτα Οὐκοῦν πατήρ ἐστιν αὐτῶν ὁ κύων Ἔγωγέ τοι εἶδον ἔφη αὐτὸν

english_head: Has he got puppies? Yes, a set of rogues like him. Then is the dog their father? Yes, indeed; I saw him with my own eyes covering the bitch. Well now, is not the dog yours? Certainly, he said. Thus he is a father, and yours, and accordingly the dog turns out to be your father, and you a brother of whelps. Hereupon Dionysodorus struck in again quickly, lest Ctesippus should get a word in before him: Answer me just one more little point: do you beat this dog? Ctesippus laughed and said: My word, yes; since I cannot beat you! So you beat your own father?

greek_head: Πολὺ μέντοι ἔφη δικαιότερον τὸν ὑμέτερον πατέρ' ἂν τύπτοιμι ὅτι μαθὼν σοφοὺς ὑεῖς οὕτως ἔφυσεν ἀλλ' ἦ

english_head: he said. There would be much more justice, though, he replied, in my beating yours, for being so ill-advised as to beget clever sons like you. Yet I doubt, Ctesippus went on, if your father, Euthydemus—the puppies’ father—has derived much good from this wisdom of yours. Why, he has no need of much good, Ctesippus, neither he nor you. And have you no need either, yourself, Euthydemus? he asked. No, nor has any other man. Just tell me, Ctesippus,

english_head: whether you think it good for a sick man to drink physic when he wants it, or whether you consider it not good; or for a man to go to the wars with arms rather than without them. With them, I think, he replied: and yet I believe you are about to utter one of your pleasantries. You will gather that well enough, he said: only answer me. Since you admit that physic is good for a man to drink when necessary, surely one ought to drink this good thing as much as possible; and in such a case it will be well to pound and infuse in it a cart-load of hellebore? To this Ctesippus replied: Quite so,

english_head: to be sure, Euthydemus, at any rate if the drinker is as big as the Delphian statue. Then, further, since in war, he proceeded, it is good to have arms, one ought to have as many spears and shields as possible, if we agree that it is a good thing? Yes, I suppose, said Ctesippus; and you, Euthydemus, do you take the other view, that it should be one shield and one spear? Yes, I do. What, he said, and would you arm Geryon also and Briareus in this way? I thought you more of an expert than that, considering you are a man-at-arms, and your comrade here too! At this Euthydemus was silent; then Dionysodorus

english_head: asked some questions on Ctesippus’ previous answers, saying: Well now, gold is in your opinion a good thing to have? Certainly, and—here I agree—plenty of it too, said Ctesippus. Well then, do you not think it right to have good things always and everywhere? Assuredly, he said. Then do you admit that gold is also a good? Why, I have admitted it, he replied. Then we ought always to have it, and everywhere, and above all, in oneself?

english_head: And one will be happiest if one has three talents of gold in one’s belly, a talent in one’s skull, and a stater of gold in each eye? Well, Euthydemus, replied Ctesippus, they say that among the Scythians those are the happiest and best men who have a lot of gold in their own skulls—somewhat as you were saying a moment ago that dog is father; and a still more marvellous thing is told, how they drink out of their skulls when gilded, and gaze inside them, holding their own headpiece in their hands. Tell me, said Euthydemus,

english_head: do the Scythians and men in general see things possible of sight, or things impossible? Possible, I presume. And you do so too? I too. Then you see our cloaks? Yes. And have they power of sight? Quite extraordinarily, said Ctesippus. What do they see? he asked. Nothing. Perhaps you do not think they see—you are such a sweet innocent. I should say, Euthydemus, that you have fallen asleep with your eyes open and, if it be possible to speak and at the same time say nothing,

greek_head: Ἦ γὰρ οὐχ οἷόν τ' ἔφη ὁ Διονυσόδωρος σιγῶντα λέγειν Οὐδ' ὁπωστιοῦν ἦ δ' ὃς ὁ Κτήσιππος Ἆρ' οὐδὲ

english_head: that this is what you are doing. Why, asked Dionysodorus, may there not be a speaking of the silent? By no means whatever, replied Ctesippus. Nor a silence of speaking? Still less, he said. Now, when you speak of stones and timbers and irons, are you not speaking of the silent? Not if I walk by a smithy, for there, as they say, the irons speak and cry aloud, when they are touched; so here your wisdom has seduced you into nonsense. But come, you have still to propound me your second point,

greek_head: Καί μοι ἐδόκει ὑπεραγωνιᾶν ὁ Κτήσιππος διὰ τὰ παιδικά Ὅταν σιγᾷς ἔφη ὁ Εὐθύδημος οὐ πάντα σιγᾷς Ἔγωγε

english_head: how on the other hand there may be a silence of speaking. (It struck me that Ctesippus was specially excited on account of his young friend’s presence.) When you are silent, said Euthydemus, are you not making a silence of all things? Yes, he replied. Then it is a silence of speaking things also, if the speaking are among all things. What, said Ctesippus, are not all things silent? I presume not, said Euthydemus. But then, my good sir, do all things speak? Yes, I suppose, at least those that speak. But that is not what I ask, he said: are all things silent or do they speak? Neither and both,

greek_head: Οὐδέτερα καὶ ἀμφότερα ἔφη ὑφαρπάσας ὁ Διονυσόδωρος εὖ γὰρ οἶδα ὅτι τῇ ἀποκρίσει οὐχ ἕξεις ὅτι χρῇ

english_head: said Dionysodorus, snatching the word from him: I am quite sure that is an answer that will baffle you! At this Ctesippus, as his manner was, gave a mighty guffaw, and said: Ah, Euthydemus, your brother has made the argument ambiguous with his both, and is worsted and done for. Then Cleinias was greatly delighted and laughed, so that Ctesippus felt his strength was as the strength of ten: but I fancy Ctesippus—he is such a rogue—had picked up these very words by overhearing the men themselves, since in nobody else of the present age is such wisdom to be found.

english_head: So I remarked: Why are you laughing, Cleinias, at such serious and beautiful things? What, have you, Socrates, ever yet seen a beautiful thing? asked Dionysodorus. Yes, I have, I replied, and many of them, Dionysodorus. Did you find them different from the beautiful, he said,

greek_head: Ἆρα ἕτερα ὄντα τοῦ καλοῦ ἔφη ἢ ταὐτὰ τῷ καλῷ Κἀγὼ ἐν παντὶ ἐγενόμην ὑπὸ ἀπορίας καὶ ἡγούμην
greek_tokens: ara eteros eimi o kalew fhmi h tautos o kalos agos en pas gignomai upo aporia kai ageomai dikaios pasxw ostis gruzw omws de eteros fhmi autos ge o kalew pareimi mentoi ekastos autos kallos tis ean oun fhmi paragignomai su bous bous ei kai ostis nun egw su pareimi dionusodwros ei eufhmew outos ge eimi de egw alla tis tropos fhmi eteros eteros paragignomai o eteros eteros an eaw
english_head: or the same as the beautiful? Here I was desperately perplexed, and felt that I had my deserts for the grunt I had made: however, I replied that they were different from the beautiful itself, though each of them had some beauty present with it. So if an ox is present with you, he said, you are an ox, and since I am now present with you, you are Dionysodorus. Heavens, do not say that! I cried. But in what way can one thing, by having a different thing present with it, be itself different?

english_head: Are you at a loss there? I asked: already I was attempting to imitate the cleverness of these men, I was so eager to get it. Can I help being at a loss, he said, I and likewise everybody else in the world, in face of what cannot be? What is that you say, Dionysodorus? I asked: is not the beautiful beautiful, and the ugly ugly? Yes, if it seems so to me, he replied. Then does it seem so? Certainly, he said. Then the same also is the same, and the different different? For I presume the different cannot be the same; nay, I thought

english_head: not even a child would doubt that the different is different. But, Dionysodorus, you have deliberately passed over this one point; though, on the whole, I feel that, like craftsmen finishing off each his special piece of work, you two are carrying out your disputation in excellent style. Well, he asked, do you know what is each craftsman’s special piece of work? First of all, whose proper task is it to forge brass? Can you tell? I can: a brazier’s. Well, again, whose to make pots? A potter’s. Once more, whose to slaughter and skin,

greek_head: Μάγειρον ἦν δ' ἐγώ Οὐκοῦν ἐάν τις ἔφη τὰ προσήκοντα πράττῃ ὀρθῶς πράξει Μάλιστα Προσήκει δέ γε
greek_tokens: mageiros eimi de egw oukoun ean tis fhmi o proshkw prassw orqos prassw malista proshkw de ge ws fhmi o mageiros katakoptw kai ekderw omologew outos h ou omologew fhmi alla suggnwmh egw exw dhlos toinun eimi de os ostis an tis sfazw o mageiros kai katakoptw eyhsis kai opthsis o proshkw poihsis kai ean o xalkeos tis autos xalkeuw kai o kerameos kerameuw kai outos o proshkw prassw
english_head: and after cutting up the joints to stew and roast? A caterer’s, I said. Now, if one does one’s proper work, he said, one will do rightly? Yes, to be sure. And is it, as you say, the caterer’s proper work to cut up and skin? Did you admit this or not? I did so, I replied, but pray forgive me. It is clear then, he proceeded, that if someone slaughters the caterer and cuts him up, and then stews or roasts him, he will be doing his proper work; and if he hammers the brazier himself, and moulds the potter, he will be doing his business likewise.

greek_head: Ὦ Πόσειδον ἦν δ' ἐγώ ἤδη κολοφῶνα ἐπιτιθεῖς τῇ σοφίᾳ ἆρά μοί ποτε αὕτη παραγενήσεται ὥστε μοι οἰκεία
greek_tokens: eimi poseidwn eimi de egw hdh kolofwn epitiqhmi o sofia ara egw pote outos paragignomai wste egw oikeios gignomai epigignwskw an autos eimi swkraths fhmi oikeios gignomai ean su ge boulomai fhmi egw dhlos ostis tis de eimi de os o sautou oiomai gignwskw ei mh tis su allos legw apo sew gar deomai arxw teleutaw de eis euqudhmos ode ara oun fhmi outos hgeomai sos eimi os an arxw kai ecesti
english_head: Poseidon! I exclaimed, there you give the finishing touch to your wisdom. I wonder if this skill could ever come to me in such manner as to be my very own. Would you recognize it, Socrates, he asked, if it came to be your own? Yes, if only you are agreeable, I replied, without a doubt. Why, he went on, do you imagine you perceive what is yours? Yes, if I take your meaning aright: for all my hopes arise from you, and end in Euthydemus here. Then tell me, he asked, do you count those things yours which you control and are free to use as you please?

greek_head: σοι αὐτοῖς χρῆσθαι ὅτι ἂν βούλῃ οἷον βοῦς καὶ πρόβατον ἆρ' ἂν ἡγοῖο ταῦτα σὰ εἶναι ἅ σοι ἐξείη καὶ ἀποδόσθαι
greek_tokens: su autos xraomai ostis an boulomai oios bous kai probaton ara an hgeomai outos sos eimi o su ecesti kai apodidwmi kai didwmi kai quw ostis boulomai qea o de an mh outws exis ou sos agos oida gar ostis ek autos kalos tis anakuptw o erwthma kai ama boulomai ostis taxistos akew panu men oun fhmi outws exis o toioutos eimi monos emos tis de zwion fhmi ou outos kalew o an yuxh exis
english_head: For instance, an ox or a sheep,would you count these as yours, if you were free to sell or bestow them, or sacrifice them to any god you chose? And things which you could not treat thus are not yours? Hereupon, since I knew that some brilliant result was sure to bob up from the mere turn of the questions, and as I also wanted to hear it as quickly as possible, I said: It is precisely as you say; only such things are mine. Well now, he went on: you call those things animals which have life?

greek_head: Ναί ἔφην Ὁμολογεῖς οὖν τῶν ζῴων ταῦτα μόνα εἶναι σά περὶ ἃ ἄν σοι ἐξουσία ᾖ πάντα ταῦτα ποιεῖν ἃ νυνδὴ
greek_tokens: nai fhmi omologew oun o zwion outos monos eimi sos peri o an su ecousia eimi pas outos poiew o nundi egw elegos omologew kai os eirwnikos panu epwxato os tis megas skopaw eipon egw fhmi eimi swkraths eimi su zeus patrwos kai egw upoptaw hkw o logos oiper teleutaw aporos tis strofh feugw te kai strefw hdh wsper en diktuon lambanw ou eimi eimi de egw eimi dionusodwros talaipwros ara tis su ge anqrwpos
english_head: Yes, I said. And you admit that only those animals are yours which you are at liberty to deal with in those various ways that I mentioned just now? I admit that. Then—after a very ironical pause, as though he were pondering some great matter—he proceeded: Tell me, Socrates, have you an ancestral Zeus? Here I suspected the discussion was approaching the point at which it eventually ended, and so I tried what desperate wriggle I could to escape from the net in which I now felt myself entangled. My answer was: I have not, Dionysodorus. What a miserable fellow you must be,

greek_head: εἶ καὶ οὐδὲ Ἀθηναῖος ᾧ μήτε θεοὶ πατρῷοί εἰσιν μήτε ἱερὰ μήτε ἄλλο μηδὲν καλὸν καὶ ἀγαθόν Ἔα ἦν δ' ἐγώ
greek_tokens: ei kai oude aqhnaios os mhte qeos patrwos eimi mhte iera mhte allos mhdeis kalos kai agaqos ea eimi de egw eimi dionusodwros eufhmew te kai mh xalepos egw prodidaskw eimi gar egw kai bwmos kai iera oikeios kai patrwos kai o allos osos o allos aqhnaia o toioutos eita o allos fhmi aqhnaia ou eimi zeus o patrwos ou eimi eimi de egw outos o epwnumios iwn oudeis oute osos ek ode o polis apoikizw eimi oute egw
english_head: he said, and no Athenian at all, if you have neither ancestral gods, nor shrines, nor anything else that denotes a gentleman! Enough, Dionysodorus; speak fair words, and don’t browbeat your pupil! For I have altars and shrines, domestic and ancestral, and everything else of the sort that other Athenians have. Then have not other Athenians, he asked, their ancestral Zeus? None of the Ionians, I replied, give him this title, neither we nor those who have left this city to settle abroad: they have an ancestral Apollo,

greek_head: ἀλλὰ Ἀπόλλων πατρῷος διὰ τὴν τοῦ Ἴωνος γένεσιν Ζεὺς δ' ἡμῖν πατρῷος μὲν οὐ καλεῖται ἕρκειος δὲ καὶ φράτριος
greek_tokens: alla apolaw patrwos dia o o iwn genesis zeus de egw patrwos men ou kalew erkeios de kai fratrios kai aqhnh fratra alla arkew ge fhmi o dionusodwros eimi gar su ws eoika apolaw te kai zeus kai aqhnh panu eimi de egw oukoun kai outos sos qeos an eien fhmi progonos eimi de egw kai despoths alla oun sos ge fhmi h ou sos omologew autos eimi omologew fhmi tis gar pasxw oukoun fhmi kai zwion eimi outos e
english_head: because of Ion’s parentage. Among us the name ancestral is not given to Zeus, but that of houseward and tribal, and we have a tribal Athena. That will do, said Dionysodorus; you have, it seems, Apollo and Zeus and Athena. Certainly, I said. Then these must be your gods? he said. My ancestors, I said, and lords. Well, at least, you have them, he said: or have you not admitted they are yours? I have admitted it, I replied: what else could I do? And are not these gods animals? he asked: you know you have admitted

greek_head: θεοί ὡμολόγηκας γὰρ ὅσα ψυχὴν ἔχει ζῷα εἶναι ἢ οὗτοι οἱ θεοὶ οὐκ ἔχουσιν ψυχήν Ἔχουσιν ἦν δ' ἐγώ Οὐκοῦν
greek_tokens: qeos omologew gar osos yuxh exis zwion eimi h outos e qeos ou exw yuxh exw eimi de egw oukoun kai zwion eimi zwion fhmi o de ge zwion fhmi omologew outos eimi sos osos an su ecesti kai didwmi kai apodidwmi kai quw dh qeaw ostis an boulomai omologew fhmi ou eimi gar egw anadusis eimi euqudhmos eimi dh egw euqus eimi de os eipon epei sos omologew eimi o dios
english_head: that whatever has life is an animal. Or have these gods no life? They have, I replied. Then are they not animals? Yes, animals, I said. And those animals, he went on, you have admitted to be yours, which you are free to bestow and sell and sacrifice to any god you please. I have admitted it, I replied; there is no escape for me, Euthydemus. Come then, tell me straight off, he said; since you admit that Zeus and the other gods are yours, are you free to sell or

greek_head: καὶ τοὺς ἄλλους θεούς ἆρα ἔξεστί σοι αὐτοὺς ἀποδόσθαι ἢ δοῦναι ἢ ἄλλ' ὅτι ἂν βούλῃ χρῆσθαι ὥσπερ τοῖς ἄλλοις
greek_tokens: kai o allos qeos ara ecesti su autos apodidwmi h didwmi h allos ostis an boulomai xraomai wsper o allos zwion egw men oun eimi kritwn wsper plhssw upo o logos keimai afwnos o de kthsippos egw eimi ws bohqew puppac eimi hraklehs fhmi kalew logos kai o dionusodwros poteros oun fhmi o hraklehs puppac eimi h o puppac hraklehs kai o kthsippos eimi poseidwn fhmi deinazw logos afisthmi amaxos o anhr
english_head: bestow them or treat them just as you please, like the other animals? Well, Crito, here I must say I was knocked out, as it were, by the argument, and lay speechless; then Ctesippus rushed to the rescue and— Bravo, Hercules! he cried, a fine argument! Whereat Dionysodorus asked: Now, do you mean that Hercules is a bravo, or that bravo is Hercules? Ctesippus replied: Poseidon, what a frightful use of words! I give up the fight: these two are invincible.

greek_head: Ἐνταῦθα μέντοι ὦ φίλε Κρίτων οὐδεὶς ὅστις οὐ τῶν παρόντων ὑπερεπῄνεσε τὸν λόγον καὶ τὼ ἄνδρε καὶ γελῶντες
greek_tokens: entauqa mentoi eimi filos kritwn oudeis ostis ou o pareimi uperepainew o logos kai o anhr kai gelaw kai krotew kai xairw oligos parateinw epi men gar o emprosqen epi ekastos pas pagkalos qorubew monos e o euqudhmos erasths entauqa de oligos kai e kiwn e en o lukeion qorubew su epi o anhr kai ezomai egw men oun kai autos outws diatiqhmi wste
english_head: Hereupon I confess, my dear Crito, that everyone present without exception wildly applauded the argument and the two men, till they all nearly died of laughing and clapping and rejoicing. For their previous successes had been highly acclaimed one by one, but only by the devotees of Euthydemus; whereas now almost the very pillars of the Lyceum took part in the joyful acclamations in honor of the pair. For myself, I was quite disposed to admit that never had I set eyes

greek_head: ὁμολογεῖν μηδένας πώποτε ἀνθρώπους ἰδεῖν οὕτω σοφούς καὶ παντάπασι καταδουλωθεὶς ὑπὸ τῆς σοφίας αὐτοῖν ἐπὶ
greek_tokens: omologew mhdeis pwpote anqrwpos eidon outws sofos kai pantapasi katadoulow upo o sofia autos epi o epainew te kai egkwmiazw autos trepw kai eipon eimi makarios sfeis o qaumastos fusis e tosoutos pragma outws taxus kai en oligos xronos ecergazomai polus men oun kai allos e logos su kalos exw eimi euqudhmos te kai dionusodwros en de o kai outos megaloprephs ostis o polus anqrwpos kai o semnon dh kai dokew
english_head: on such clever people, and I was so utterly enthralled by their skill that I betook myself to praising and congratulating them, and said: Ah, happy pair! What amazing genius, to acquire such a great accomplishment so quickly and in so short a time! Among the many fine points in your arguments, Euthydemus and Dionysodorus, there is one that stands out in particular magnificence—that you care not a jot for the multitude, or for any would-be important or famous people, but only for those of your own sort. And I am perfectly sure that there are but a few persons

greek_head: τὶ εἶναι οὐδὲν ὑμῖν μέλει ἀλλὰ τῶν ὁμοίων ὑμῖν μόνον ἐγὼ γὰρ εὖ οἶδα ὅτι τούτους τοὺς λόγους πάνυ μὲν ἂν ὀλίγοι
greek_tokens: tis eimi oudeis su melos alla o omoios su monos egw gar eu oida ostis outos o logos panu men an oligos agapaw anqrwpos omoios su e de allos outws agnoew autos wste eu oida ostis aisxunw an mallon ecelegxw toioutos logos o allos h autos ecelegxw kai ode au eteros dhmotikos tis kai praos en o logos opotan fhmi mhte kalos eimi mhdeis mhte agaqos pragma mhte leukon mhde allos o toioutos mhdeis mhde o parapan
english_head: like yourselves who would be satisfied with these arguments: the rest of the world regard them only as arguments with which, I assure you, they would feel it a greater disgrace to refute others than to be refuted themselves. And further, there is at the same time a popular and kindly feature in your talk: when you say there is nothing either beautiful, or good, or white, and so on, and no difference of things at all, in truth you simply stitch up men’s mouths,

greek_head: ἑτέρων ἕτερον ἀτεχνῶς μὲν τῷ ὄντι συρράπτετε τὰ στόματα τῶν ἀνθρώπων ὥσπερ καὶ φατέ ὅτι δ' οὐ μόνον τὰ τῶν
greek_tokens: eteros eteros atexnos men o eimi surraptw o stoma o anqrwpos wsper kai fatos ostis de ou monos o o allos alla dokew an kai o umeteros autos outos panu xarieis su eimi kai o epaxqhs o logos afairew o de dh megas ostis outos outws exis su kai texnikos eceuriskw wste en panu oligos xronos ostis an manqanw anqrwpos gignwskw egw kai o kthsippos o new prosexw ws taxus su ek o paraxrhma mimeomai oios te eimi
english_head: as you expressly say you do; while as to your apparent power of stitching up your own mouths as well, this is a piece of agreeable manners that takes off any offence from your talk. But the greatest thing of all is, that this faculty of yours is such, and is so skilfully contrived, that anyone in the world may learn it of you in a very short time; this fact I perceived myself by watching Ctesippus and observing how quickly he was able to imitate you on the spot. Now, in so far as your accomplishment can be quickly imparted,

greek_head: τοῦτο μὲν οὖν τοῦ πράγματος σφῶν τὸ σοφὸν πρὸς μὲν τὸ ταχὺ παραδιδόναι καλόν ἐναντίον δ' ἀνθρώπων διαλέγεσθαι
greek_tokens: outos men oun o pragma sfazw o sofos pros men o taxus paradidwmi kalos enantion de anqrwpos dialegw ou epithdeios alla an ge egw peiqw eulabeomai mh polus enantion legw ina mh taxus ekmanqanw su mh oida xaris alla malista men autos pros allhlwn monos dialegw ei de mh eiper allos tis enantion ekeinos monos os an su didwmi argurion o autos de outos ean
english_head: it is excellent; but for public discussions it is not suitable: if I may advise you, beware of talking before a number of people, lest they learn the whole thing in a trice and give you no credit for it. The best thing for you is to talk to each other by yourselves, in private; failing that, if a third person is present, it must be someone who will pay you a good fee. And if you are prudent

greek_head: σωφρονῆτε καὶ τοῖς μαθηταῖς συμβουλεύσετε μηδέποτε μηδενὶ ἀνθρώπων διαλέγεσθαι ἀλλ' ἢ ὑμῖν τε καὶ αὑτοῖς
greek_tokens: swfronew kai o maqhths sumbouleuw mhdepote mhdeis anqrwpos dialegw alla h su te kai eautou o gar spanios eimi euqudhmos timios o de udwr euwnos ariston eimi ws fhmi pindaros alla age eimi de egw opws amos kai kleinihs ode paradexomai outos eimi kritwn kai allos atta eti braxus dialegw apeimi skopaw oun opws sumfoithsis para o
english_head: you will give this same counsel to your pupils also—that they are never to converse with anybody except you and each other. For it is the rare, Euthydemus, that is precious, while water is cheapest, though best, as Pindar said. But come, I said, see if you can admit both me and Cleinias here to your class. This, Crito, was our conversation, and after exchanging a few more words we went off. Now you must arrange to join us in taking lessons from the pair;

greek_head: ἄνδρε ὡς ἐκείνω φατὸν οἵω τε εἶναι διδάξαι τὸν ἐθέλοντ' ἀργύριον διδόναι καὶ οὔτε φύσιν οὔθ' ἡλικίαν ἐξείργειν
greek_tokens: anhr ws ekeinos fatos oios te eimi didaskw o eqelw argurion didwmi kai oute fus oute hlikia ecergw oudeis o de kai sos malista proshkw akew ostis oude o xrhmatizw fhmi diakwluw oudeis mh ou paralambanw ostis eupeths o sfeteros sofia kai meis eimi swkraths filhkoos men egw kai hdews an tis manqanw kinduneuw mentoi agos eis eimi o ou omoios euqudhmos alla ekeinos os dh kai su
english_head: for they say they are able to teach anyone who is willing to pay good money, and that no sort of character or age—and it is well that you especially should be told that they promise that their art is no hindrance to money-making—need deter anyone from an easy acquisition of their wisdom. Indeed, Socrates, I love listening, and would be glad to learn from them; but I am afraid I am one of the sort who are not like Euthydemus, but who, as you described them just now, would prefer being refuted

greek_head: ἔλεγες τῶν ἥδιον ἂν ἐξελεγχομένων ὑπὸ τῶν τοιούτων λόγων ἢ ἐξελεγχόντων ἀτὰρ γελοῖον μέν μοι δοκεῖ εἶναι
greek_tokens: legw o hdus an ecelegxw upo o toioutos logos h ecelegxw atar geloios men egw dokew eimi o nouqetew su omws de o ge akouw eqelw su apaggellw o apo su apeimi eimi ostis proserxomai tis egw peripatew anhr oiomai panu eimi sofos outos tis o peri o logos o eis o dikasthrion deinazw eimi kritwn fhmi oudeis akroaomai ode o sofos ou ma o dios eimi de egw ou gar oios su eimi prosisthmi katakouw upo o oxlos kai meis fhmi acios ge eimi akew tis
english_head: to refuting with such arguments. Now, although I feel it is absurd to admonish you, I wish nevertheless to report to you what was told me just now. Do you know, one of the people who had left your discussion came up to me as I was taking a stroll—a man who thinks himself very wise, one of those who are so clever at turning out speeches for the law-courts—and said: Crito, do you take no lessons from these wise men? No, in truth, I replied: there was such a crowd that, though I stood quite close, I was unable to catch what was said. Well, let me tell you, he said, it was something worth hearing.

greek_head: δέ ἦν δ' ἐγώ Ἵνα ἤκουσας ἀνδρῶν διαλεγομένων οἳ νῦν σοφώτατοί εἰσι τῶν περὶ τοὺς τοιούτους λόγους Κἀγὼ
greek_tokens: de eimi de egw ina akouw androw dialegw e nun sofos eimi o peri o toioutos logos agos eipon tis oun fainw su tis de allos eimi de os h oios aei an tis o toioutos aekwn lhrew kai peri oudeis acios anacios spoudh poiew outwsi gar pws kai eipon o onoma kai egw alla mentoi fhmi xarieis ge tis pragma eimi o filosofia poios fhmi xarieis eimi
english_head: What was it? I asked. You would have heard the disputation of men who are the most accomplished of our day in that kind of speaking. To this I replied: Well, what did they show forth to you? Merely the sort of stuff, he said, that you may hear such people babbling about at any time—making an inconsequent ado about matters of no consequence (in some such parlance he expressed himself). Whereupon—Well, all the same, I said, philosophy is a charming thing. Charming is it, my dear innocent? he exclaimed:

greek_head: μακάριε οὐδενὸς μὲν οὖν ἄξιον ἀλλὰ καὶ εἰ νῦν παρεγένου πάνυ ἄν σε οἶμαι αἰσχυνθῆναι ὑπὲρ τοῦ σεαυτοῦ ἑταίρου
greek_tokens: makarios oudeis men oun acios alla kai ei nun paragignomai panu an su oimh aisxunw uper o sautou etairos outws eimi atopos eqelw eautou parexw anqrwpos os oudeis melos ostis an legw pas de rhma antexw kai outos osper arti elegos en o kratistos eimi o nun alla gar eimi kritwn fhmi o pragma autos kai e anqrwpos e epi o pragma diatribw faulos eimi kai katagelastos egw de eimi swkraths o pragma dokew
english_head: nay, a thing of no consequence. Why, had you been in that company just now, you would have been filled with shame, I fancy, for your particular friend: he was so strangely willing to lend himself to persons who care not a straw what they say, but merely fasten on any phrase that turns up. And these, as I said just now, are the heads of their profession today. But the fact is, Crito, he went on, the business itself and the people who follow it are worthless and ridiculous. Now, in my opinion, Socrates, he was not right in decrying the pursuit:

greek_head: οὐκ ὀρθῶς ψέγειν οὔθ' οὗτος οὔτ' εἴ τις ἄλλος ψέγει τὸ μέντοι ἐθέλειν διαλέγεσθαι τοιούτοις ἐναντίον πολλῶν
greek_tokens: ou orqos yegw oute outos oute ei tis allos yegw o mentoi eqelw dialegw toioutos enantion polus anqrwpos orqos egw dokew memfomai eimi kritwn qaumasios eimi e toioutos anhr atar oupw oida ostis mellw erew poteros eimi o proserxomai su kai memfomai o filosofia poteros o agwnizomai deinazw en o dikasthrion rhtwr tis h o o toioutos eispempw poihths o logos os e rhtwr agwnizomai
english_head: he is wrong, and so is anyone else who decries it: though I must say I felt he was right in blaming the readiness to engage in discussion with such people before a large company. Crito, these people are very odd. But I do not yet know what answer I shall give you. Of which party was he who came up to you and blamed philosophy? Was he one of those who excel in the contests of the courts, an orator; or of those who equip the orators for the fray, a composer of the speeches they deliver in their contests?

greek_head: Ἥκιστα νὴ τὸν Δία ῥήτωρ οὐδὲ οἶμαι πώποτ' αὐτὸν ἐπὶ δικαστήριον ἀναβεβηκέναι ἀλλ' ἐπαΐειν αὐτόν φασι
greek_tokens: hkistos nh o dios rhtwr oude oimh pwpote autos epi dikasthrion anabainw alla epaiw autos fhmi peri o pragma nh o dios kai deinos eimi kai deinos logos suntiqhmi hdh manqanw peri outos kai autos nundi mellw legw outos gar eimi men eimi kritwn os fhmi prodikos meqorios filosofos te anhr kai politikos oiomai de eimi pas sofos anqrwpos pros de o eimi kai dokew panu para polus wste para pas eudokimew
english_head: Nothing of an orator, I dare swear, nor do I think he has ever appeared in court: only he is reputed to know about the business, so they declare, and to be a clever person, and compose clever speeches. Now I understand: it was of these people that I was just now going to speak myself. They are the persons, Crito, whom Prodicus described as the border-ground between philosopher and politician, yet they fancy that they are the wisest of all mankind, and that they not merely are but are thought so by a great many people; and accordingly

greek_head: ἐμποδὼν σφίσιν εἶναι οὐδένας ἄλλους ἢ τοὺς περὶ φιλοσοφίαν ἀνθρώπους ἡγοῦνται οὖν ἐὰν τούτους εἰς δόξαν
greek_tokens: empodwn sfeis eimi oudeis allos h o peri filosofia anqrwpos hgeomai oun ean outos eis doca kaqisthmi mhdeis dokew acios eimi anamfisbhthtos hdh para pas o nikhthrios eis doca ferw sofia peri eimi men gar o alhqeia sfazw sofos en de o idios logos otan apolambanw upo o amfi euqudhmos kolouw sofos de hgeomai eimi panu eoikotws metrios men gar filosofia exw metrios de politikos
english_head: they feel that none but the followers of philosophy stand in the way of their universal renown. Hence they believe that, if they can reduce the latter to a status of no esteem, the prize of victory will by common consent be awarded to them, without dispute or delay, and their claim to wisdom will be won. For they consider themselves to be in very truth the wisest, but find that, when caught in private conversation, they are cut off short by Euthydemus and his set. This conceit of their wisdom is very natural, since they regard themselves as moderately versed in philosophy, and moderately too in politics, on quite reasonable grounds:

greek_head: πάνυ ἐξ εἰκότος λόγου μετέχειν γὰρ ἀμφοτέρων ὅσον ἔδει ἐκτὸς δὲ ὄντες κινδύνων καὶ ἀγώνων καρποῦσθαι τὴν σοφίαν
greek_tokens: panu ek eoika logos metexw gar amfoteros osos dew ektos de eimi kindunos kai agwnos karpow o sofia tis oun dokew sos tis eimi swkraths legw ou gar su alla o ge logos exis tis euprepeia o androw kai gar exis ontws eimi kritwn euprepeia mallon
english_head: for they have dipped into both as far as they needed, and, evading all risk and struggle, are content to gather the fruits of wisdom. Well, now, do you consider, Socrates, that there is anything in what they say? It is not to be denied that these men have some color for their statements. Yes, that is so, Crito; color rather than truth.

greek_head: ἢ ἀλήθειαν οὐ γὰρ ῥᾴδιον αὐτοὺς πεῖσαι ὅτι καὶ ἄνθρωποι καὶ τἆλλα πάντα ὅσα μεταξύ τινοιν δυοῖν ἐστιν καὶ ἀμφοτέροιν
greek_tokens: h alhqeia ou gar radios autos peiqw ostis kai anqrwpos kai allos pas osos metacu tis duo eimi kai amfoteros tugxanw metexw osos men ek kakos kai agaqos o men beltiwn o de xeirwn gignomai osos de ek duo agaqos mh pros tautos amfw xeirwn pros o an ekateros eimi xrhstos ekeinos ek os suntiqhmi osos de ek duo kakos suntiqhmi mh pros o autos eimi en o mesos eimi outos
english_head: It is no easy matter to persuade them that either people or things, which are between two other things and have a certain share of both, if compounded of bad and good are found to be better than the one and worse than the other; but if compounded of two good things which have not the same object, they are worse than either of their components in relation to the object to which each of them is adapted; while if they are compounded of two bad things which have not the same object, and stand between them, this is the only case

greek_head: μόνα βελτίω ἑκατέρου ἐκείνων ἐστίν ὧν ἀμφοτέρων μέρος μετέχουσιν εἰ μὲν οὖν ἡ φιλοσοφία ἀγαθόν ἐστιν καὶ ἡ
greek_tokens: monos beltiwn ekateros ekeinos eimi os amfoteros meros metexw ei men oun o filosofia agaqos eimi kai o politikos pracis pros allos de ekateros outos de amfoteros metexw outos en mesos eimi oudeis legw amfoteros gar eimi faulos ei de agaqos kai kakos o men beltiwn o de xeirwn ei de kakos amfoteros outws an tis legw alhqhs allos de oudamos ou an oun oimh
english_head: where they are better than either of the two things of which they have a share. Now if philosophy and the statesman’s business are both good things, and each of them has a different object, and if these persons, partaking of both, are between them, their claims are nought; for they are inferior to both: if one is good and the other bad, they are better than the one and worse than the other: while if both are bad, in this case there would be some truth in their statement, but in any other case there is none. Now I do not think they will admit

greek_head: αὐτοὺς ὁμολογῆσαι οὔτε κακὼ αὐτὼ ἀμφοτέρω εἶναι οὔτε τὸ μὲν κακόν τὸ δὲ ἀγαθόν ἀλλὰ τῷ ὄντι οὗτοι ἀμφοτέρων
greek_tokens: autos omologew oute kakos autos amfoteros eimi oute o men kakos o de agaqos alla o eimi outos amfoteros metexw amfoteros hsswn eimi pros ekateros pros o o te politikos kai o filosofia acios logos eimi kai tritos eimi o alhqeia zhtew proteros dokew eimi suggignwskw men oun autos xrh o epiqumia kai mh xalepainw hgeomai mentoi toioutos eimi oios eimi pas gar anhr xrh agaph ostis kai otioun legw exw
english_head: either that both these things are bad, or that one is bad and the other good: the truth is that these people, partaking of both, are inferior to both in respect of the objects for which statesmanship and philosophy are important; and while they are really in the third place they seek to be accorded the first. However, we ought to be indulgent towards their ambition and not feel annoyed, while still judging them to be what they actually are. For we should be glad of anyone, whoever he may be, who says anything that verges on good sense, and labours steadily

greek_head: φρονήσεως πρᾶγμα καὶ ἀνδρείως ἐπεξιὼν διαπονεῖται Καὶ μήν ὦ Σώκρατες καὶ αὐτὸς περὶ τῶν ὑέων
greek_tokens: fronhsis pragma kai andreios epeceimi diaponew kai meis eimi swkraths kai autos peri o uios wsper aei pros su legos en aporia eimi tis deomai autos xraw o men oun neos eti kai mikros eimi kritoboulos de hdh hlikia exis kai deomai tis ostis autos onhsis egw men oun otan sos suggignomai outws diatiqhmi wste egw dokew mania eimi o eneka o pais allos men polus spoudh toioutos exw kai peri
english_head: and manfully in its pursuit. Now I myself, Socrates, as I so often tell you, am in doubt about my sons, as to what I am to do with them. The younger is as yet quite small; but Critobulus is already grown up, and needs someone who will be of service to him. When I am in your company, the effect on me is such as to make me feel it is mere madness to have taken ever so much pains in various directions for the good of my children—

greek_head: τοῦ γάμου ὅπως ἐκ γενναιοτάτης ἔσονται μητρός καὶ περὶ τῶν χρημάτων ὅπως ὡς πλουσιώτατοι αὐτῶν δὲ περὶ
greek_tokens: o gamos opws ek gennaios eimi mhthr kai peri o xrhma opws ws plousios autos de peri paideia amelew otan de eis tis apoblepw o faskw an paideuw anqrwpos ekplhssw kai egw dokew eis ekastos autos skopaw panu allokotos eimi
english_head: first in so marrying that they should be of very good blood on their mother’s side; then in making money so that they might be as well off as possible; while I have neglected the training of the boys themselves. But when I glance at one of the persons who profess to educate people, I am dismayed, and feel that each one of them, when I consider them, is wholly unsuitable—

greek_head: ὥς γε πρὸς σὲ τἀληθῆ εἰρῆσθαι ὥστε οὐκ ἔχω ὅπως προτρέπω τὸ μειράκιον ἐπὶ φιλοσοφίαν
greek_tokens: os ge pros sos alhqhs erw wste ou exw opws protrepw o meirakion epi filosofia eimi filos kritwn ou oida ostis en pas epithdeuma e men faulos polus kai oudeis acios e de spoudaios oligos kai pas acios epei gumnastikos ou kalos dokew su eimi kai xrhmatistikos kai rhtorikos kai strathgia egw pantws dhpou tis oun en ekastos outos o polus pros
english_head: to tell you the truth between ourselves. So that I cannot see how I am to incline the lad towards philosophy. My dear Crito are you not aware that in every trade the duffers are many and worthless, whereas the good workers are few and worth any price? Why, do you not hold athletics, and money-making, and rhetoric, and generalship, to be fine things? Certainly I do, of course.

greek_head: ἕκαστον τὸ ἔργον οὐ καταγελάστους ὁρᾷς Ναὶ μὰ τὸν Δία καὶ μάλα ἀληθῆ λέγεις
greek_tokens: ekastos o ergnumi ou katagelastos oraw nai ma o dios kai mala alhqhs legw eimi oun outos eneka autos te feugw pas o epithdeuma kai o uios ou epitrepw oukoun dikaios ge eimi swkraths mh toinun o ge ou xrh poiew eimi kritwn alla eaw xairw o epithdeuw filosofia eite xrhstos eimi eite ponhros autos o pragma basanizw kalos te kai eu
english_head: Well then, in each of these, do you not see most men making a ridiculous show at their respective tasks? Yes, I know: what you say is perfectly true. Then will you yourself on this account eschew all these pursuits, and not let your son have anything to do with them? No, there would be no good reason for that, Socrates. Then avoid at least what is wrong, Crito: let those who practise philosophy have their way,

greek_head: ἐὰν μέν σοι φαίνηται φαῦλον ὄν πάντ' ἄνδρα ἀπότρεπε μὴ μόνον τοὺς ὑεῖς ἐὰν δὲ φαίνηται οἷον οἶμαι αὐτὸ ἐγὼ
greek_tokens: ean men su fainw faulos eimi panth anhr apotrepw mh monos o uios ean de fainw oios oimh autos egw eimi qarsos diwkw kai askew o legw dh outos autos te kai o paidia
english_head: whether they are helpful or mischievous; and when you have tested the matter itself, well and truly, if you find it to be a poor affair, turn everyone you can away from it, not only your sons: but if you find it to be such as I think it is, pursue and ply it without fear, both you, as they say, and yours.
